questions.question_text:
You have delayed labels: the ground-truth outcome arrives 7–30 days after prediction (e.g., chargeback fraud). You also collect user feedback (manual review outcomes) that can be noisy.

Design a data/label quality operations loop that:
- Tracks label completeness and correctness over time
- Prevents training on partial labels incorrectly
- Incorporates human review feedback without contaminating ground truth
- Supports re-labeling and dataset versioning
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer proposes:
- Separate concepts: (a) prediction events (scored_at, features, model_version), (b) provisional labels (manual review, heuristics), (c) final ground truth labels (arrive later), each with source and timestamp.
- Completeness tracking: for each cohort (scored_date), track % with final labels received, distribution of delay, and label arrival SLAs; dashboards by segment.
- Training data construction: use a “label cutoff” policy (only train on examples older than N days where label completeness > threshold) and/or survival/positive-unlabeled strategies if needed. Explicitly exclude examples with unknown final labels unless modeling censoring.
- Noise handling: treat human review as a separate signal; use it for triage, active learning, or weak supervision, but keep it distinct from ground truth. If it becomes ground truth, define adjudication and audit.
- Versioning: dataset snapshots with immutable manifests (feature set version, label extraction code version, cutoff date). Support relabeling by creating new dataset versions; track diffs and provenance.
- Feedback loops: sample disagreements between review and eventual ground truth; measure reviewer accuracy and drift; route hard cases to adjudication; update guidelines.

This design ensures point-in-time correctness and avoids training on partially observed outcomes.
questions.explanation: Delayed labels are a common production reality. The key is to model label lifecycle explicitly and build training datasets with a principled cutoff to avoid bias and leakage. Keeping provisional signals separate from final ground truth preserves evaluability and avoids contaminating labels. Dataset versioning and provenance make retraining reproducible and allow controlled relabeling/backfills.

questions.question_text:
For an LLM agent that calls tools (search, DB lookup, ticket creation), which observability setup best supports debugging a single bad user outcome end-to-end?

A. Only log the final user prompt and the final model response.
B. Log token counts and average latency per endpoint.
C. Use distributed tracing with a trace_id propagated through the gateway, LLM calls, retrieval/tool calls, and post-processing; log prompts/tool inputs/outputs with redaction and version metadata.
D. Sample 1% of requests and store full raw logs without any metadata to reduce cost.
options: A. Only log the final user prompt and the final model response.
B. Log token counts and average latency per endpoint.
C. Use distributed tracing with a trace_id propagated through the gateway, LLM calls, retrieval/tool calls, and post-processing; log prompts/tool inputs/outputs with redaction and version metadata.
D. Sample 1% of requests and store full raw logs without any metadata to reduce cost.
questions.explanation: End-to-end debugging requires correlating all steps for a single request: prompt construction, model version, retrieved context, tool calls, and post-processing. Distributed tracing with propagated IDs enables this. You still need redaction and access control for sensitive data. Aggregate metrics (B) are useful but insufficient for root cause analysis of individual failures.

questions.question_text:
You’re defining an API for an embedding + vector search service used by multiple product teams. You need to frame requirements and design the interface so ML changes don’t break clients.

Write a concise API proposal (endpoints and request/response fields) that supports:
- Creating embeddings (multiple models)
- Upserting documents with metadata into a vector index
- Querying with filters and returning citations
- Versioning (embedding model + index schema)
- Rate limits and error handling

Also state 3 non-functional requirements you would clarify with the product team.
questions.explanation: This tests whether the candidate can translate ML capabilities into stable product interfaces. Versioning fields prevent silent behavior changes when embedding models or index schemas evolve. Trace IDs and idempotency are practical necessities in production. Non-functional requirements drive architecture (caching, batching, async ingestion, tenancy isolation).

questions.question_text:
Design the feedback collection system for an LLM app where users can: thumbs up/down, highlight a problematic span, and optionally edit the answer. Requirements:
- Must tie feedback to the exact prompt/context, model version, and retrieval results
- Must support later offline analysis and training dataset creation
- Must be privacy-aware (PII) and allow deletion requests
- Should support near-real-time dashboards for quality

Describe the event schema and storage/processing architecture. How would you ensure traceability from a production response to training examples?
questions.correct_answer:
A good answer proposes:
- Event schema: response_id (stable), conversation_id, user_id/tenant_id (hashed), timestamp, model_id+version, prompt template version, full prompt/context (or references), retrieval metadata (doc_ids, chunk_ids, scores, index version), tool calls, final response text, safety filters applied, latency/cost, and feedback fields (thumb, reason tag, span offsets, edited_text, free-text comment). Include client/app version.
- Storage: append-only event log (Kafka/Kinesis/PubSub) -> raw immutable store (data lake) + serving store for dashboards (OLAP like BigQuery/Snowflake/ClickHouse). Maintain a trace store keyed by response_id with all artifacts.
- Privacy: tokenize/redact PII at ingestion, store raw text in a restricted enclave, apply RBAC and audit logs; implement deletion via tombstones + reprocessing or keyed deletion in serving stores; minimize retention of raw sensitive fields.
- Traceability: make response_id the join key across traces, retrieval, and feedback; store exact model/retrieval versions; snapshot prompt template; optionally store the feature vector / retrieved passages used. Build an offline “training example builder” job that materializes (input, preferred output/edited output, metadata) with provenance pointers.
- Dashboards: stream aggregates (thumb rate, reason tags, per-segment) with near-real-time pipelines; keep raw for deeper investigation.
questions.explanation: The key is end-to-end provenance: without model version, prompt template version, and retrieval snapshot, feedback can’t be reliably used for training or debugging. Strong candidates think in terms of immutable logs + derived tables, and explicitly handle privacy and deletion—common real-world constraints for LLM products.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an LLM-based support assistant. You want to collect human feedback that can be used for model improvement. Which feedback schema is most useful for training preference models / RLHF-style tuning while still being easy for agents?

A. Only a thumbs up/down per conversation.
B. Free-text comments only.
C. Capture the final chosen response among 2+ candidate responses (pairwise or listwise), plus optional edit and reason tags.
D. Log only whether the user returned within 7 days (implicit feedback).
options: A. Only a thumbs up/down per conversation.
B. Free-text comments only.
C. Capture the final chosen response among 2+ candidate responses (pairwise or listwise), plus optional edit and reason tags.
D. Log only whether the user returned within 7 days (implicit feedback).
questions.explanation: Preference training needs comparative signals (which answer is better) more than absolute ratings. Pairwise/listwise choices create clean training data; edits provide supervised targets; reason tags help slice errors. Thumbs-only (A) is noisy and confounded; comments-only (B) are hard to structure; implicit retention (D) is weakly attributable and delayed.

questions.question_text:
You run a daily batch pipeline that recomputes features and trains a model. A proposed feature change requires a 2-year backfill and could shift label alignment. Describe how you would do impact analysis before committing to the backfill and rollout. Include:
- What datasets you would compare (offline vs online, old vs new)
- What metrics you would compute (overall + segment)
- How you would detect label leakage or time-travel bugs
- A rollout plan that reduces blast radius
questions.correct_answer:
A solid answer includes:
- Dataset comparisons: produce a paired dataset with identical entity/time keys containing both v1 and v2 feature values; compare distributions, missingness, and join rates. If possible, replay a slice of online events to compute v2 as it would be online and compare to offline computation.
- Metrics: feature-level drift (KS/PSI), correlation changes, null rate deltas; model-level offline eval (AUC/PR, calibration) for v1 vs v2; segment metrics by key slices (geo, device, new vs returning, high-risk cohorts), plus stability metrics (variance over time).
- Leakage/time-travel: verify point-in-time correctness (feature timestamps <= prediction time), enforce watermarking, run unit tests on window boundaries, and do “future-shift” checks (if using future data improves metrics suspiciously). Validate label alignment rules explicitly.
- Rollout: ship v2 as parallel feature, train shadow model, run shadow inference to compare decisions, then canary rollout to small traffic, monitor segment-level regressions, and only then migrate fully; keep rollback path to v1 and keep both features for a deprecation window.
questions.explanation: Backfills are expensive and risky; impact analysis reduces wasted compute and production incidents. The key is paired comparisons (same keys), point-in-time validation to avoid leakage, and staged rollout (shadow/canary) with segment monitoring. Good candidates also mention join-rate changes and missingness as common sources of hidden regressions.

questions.question_text:
Your team wants to change a feature definition used by multiple models (e.g., redefine “active_user_7d”). You must backfill historical values for training and ensure online/offline consistency. Which plan is safest?

A. Update the SQL in place and rerun training; if metrics look good, deploy.
B. Create a new versioned feature (active_user_7d_v2), backfill it, run impact analysis and shadow evaluation, then migrate consumers and deprecate v1.
C. Only change the online feature; offline training can keep the old definition to avoid backfills.
D. Ship the change behind a runtime flag but do not backfill; models will adapt after retraining later.
options: A. Update the SQL in place and rerun training; if metrics look good, deploy.
B. Create a new versioned feature (active_user_7d_v2), backfill it, run impact analysis and shadow evaluation, then migrate consumers and deprecate v1.
C. Only change the online feature; offline training can keep the old definition to avoid backfills.
D. Ship the change behind a runtime flag but do not backfill; models will adapt after retraining later.
questions.explanation: Versioning + backfill + impact analysis is the safest because it prevents silent breaking changes and allows side-by-side comparison. (A) risks hidden consumer breakage and irreproducible training. (C) creates online/offline skew. (D) introduces inconsistent semantics and makes debugging regressions difficult.

questions.question_text:
You need to deliver model explainability in production for a tabular fraud model used by support agents and also for internal ML debugging. Requirements:
- User-facing: show top reasons per decision, stable wording, no leakage of sensitive features
- Internal: deeper feature contributions, raw feature values, and links to training data slices
- Must work at low latency (p95 < 150ms) and be consistent with the exact model version used

Design an explainability delivery approach (APIs, caching/precompute vs on-demand, versioning, and access control). Mention at least one risk and mitigation.
questions.correct_answer:
A good design separates explainability artifacts into (1) a lightweight, user-safe explanation payload and (2) a privileged internal payload. Common approach:
- Online inference returns: prediction + model_version + explanation_id (or inline top-k contributions if cheap).
- Compute explanations either (a) on-demand using fast methods (e.g., linear/GBDT contributions) or (b) asynchronously for expensive methods (e.g., SHAP) and store results keyed by (entity_id, event_time, model_version, feature_set_version).
- Provide an Explanation Service API: GET /explanations/{explanation_id} with RBAC; user-facing endpoint filters/aggregates features into stable “reason codes” and templates; internal endpoint returns full contributions, raw features, and slice links.
- Versioning: include model hash + feature definitions version; store explanation artifacts with these versions to ensure consistency.
- Latency: precompute reason codes at inference time (cheap) and optionally cache; for expensive explanations, return immediate reason codes and make deep explanations available asynchronously.
- Access control: strict allowlist of features exposed to users; internal access behind auth, audit logs.

Risk: explanation/model mismatch due to feature drift or model updates; mitigate by binding explanations to model_version and feature_set_version, and by storing the exact feature vector used for scoring (or sufficient provenance) for internal debugging.
questions.explanation: This checks practical explainability delivery, not theoretical XAI. The core is productizing explanations with versioning, RBAC, and latency constraints. Strong answers distinguish “reason codes” (stable, safe, comprehensible) from raw feature attributions (debuggable but sensitive), and ensure explanations are tied to the exact model and feature pipeline used.

questions.question_text:
In an annotation pipeline with labelers and reviewers, which approach best prevents double-labeling while keeping workers busy when clients crash or lose connectivity?

A. Assign tasks by writing the worker_id into the task row permanently; if the worker disconnects, a manager manually reassigns.
B. Use a FIFO queue; when a worker pops a task it is removed from the queue immediately.
C. Use lease-based assignment: worker requests a task, server marks it as leased with an expiration; if not completed by expiry, it becomes available again.
D. Let workers pick tasks from a shared list; rely on front-end locking in the browser tab to prevent collisions.
options: A. Assign tasks by writing the worker_id into the task row permanently; if the worker disconnects, a manager manually reassigns.
B. Use a FIFO queue; when a worker pops a task it is removed from the queue immediately.
C. Use lease-based assignment: worker requests a task, server marks it as leased with an expiration; if not completed by expiry, it becomes available again.
D. Let workers pick tasks from a shared list; rely on front-end locking in the browser tab to prevent collisions.
questions.explanation: Lease-based assignment is the standard pattern for human-in-the-loop queues: it avoids permanent assignment (A), avoids losing tasks on crash (B), and avoids relying on fragile client-side locks (D). With leases you can also measure timeouts, detect stuck work, and tune lease durations by task type.

questions.question_text:
You’re asked to design an internal data annotation + review tool for creating training labels for a vision model. Requirements:
- 5k images/day initially, scaling to 200k/day
- Multiple labelers, then QA review, then adjudication for disagreements
- Need per-item audit trail and ability to re-open items
- Want to minimize idle time for labelers/reviewers

Describe a system design at a high level (components + data model + workflow). Specifically address: (1) how you represent task states, (2) how you implement queues/assignment, (3) how you handle disagreements and adjudication, and (4) how you measure throughput and quality.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer proposes a workflow-oriented system with explicit state machine and separate artifacts for labels vs. tasks. Typical components: (a) Task service (creates tasks, manages state transitions), (b) Assignment/queue service (pull-based or lease-based), (c) Label storage (immutable label submissions), (d) Review service (QA + adjudication), (e) UI, (f) Metrics/analytics.

(1) Task states: e.g., NEW -> LABELED -> IN_QA -> QA_PASSED or QA_FAILED -> (if failed) RELABEL or ADJUDICATION -> DONE. Use a state machine with allowed transitions; store who/when/why for each transition (audit log).

(2) Queues/assignment: use a pull model where clients request the next task; server grants a lease (task_id, expires_at) to avoid double work. Maintain separate queues by skill, priority, dataset slice, or language. Use idempotent completion endpoints; on lease expiry, return to queue.

(3) Disagreements/adjudication: store each labeler’s submission as an immutable record; compute agreement metrics (IoU, class match, span overlap, etc.) and trigger adjudication when disagreement > threshold or QA flags. Adjudicator can choose one submission, merge, or create a final “gold” label. Keep the final label linked to source submissions.

(4) Throughput/quality: measure cycle time per stage, queue depth, lease timeouts, per-user throughput, disagreement rate, QA pass rate, rework rate, and inter-annotator agreement. Add sampling-based QA and targeted QA for hard segments. Provide dashboards and alerts for backlog and quality regressions.
questions.explanation: This tests whether the candidate can design labeling as an operations system, not just a UI. Key ideas: explicit state machine (prevents ambiguous lifecycle), lease-based assignment (prevents duplicate work and reduces idling), immutable submissions + final adjudicated label (auditability and reprocessing), and stage-level metrics (to manage throughput and quality). Strong designs also separate “task” (work unit) from “label” (artifact), enabling reopens/backfills without losing provenance.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing an LLM agent that can call internal tools (refunds, account changes). The system will be used by support agents and sometimes directly by end users.

Design practical safety controls against prompt injection and unsafe tool use. Include:
- How you separate untrusted user content from system/tool instructions
- Tool-call authorization and validation (schema + policy)
- Output filtering and human-in-the-loop escalation
- Red teaming / continuous testing workflow you’d implement
- What you log for incident response
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer layers defenses: strict message role separation (system/developer vs user), never allowing user text to modify tool specs; retrieval content is treated as untrusted and quoted. Tool use guardrails: allowlist tools per tenant/role, require authentication/authorization, validate tool-call JSON against schemas, enforce policy checks (e.g., refund limits, account ownership), and use a separate policy engine before execution. It uses constrained decoding/tool-call mode where possible, and rejects/repairs invalid calls. Output safety: PII redaction, policy filters, and escalation to human approval for high-risk actions (refunds, account changes) or low confidence. Red teaming: maintain an adversarial prompt suite, run CI evaluations on prompt/tool changes, monitor jailbreak rates, and periodically run manual exercises. Logging: full trace with message IDs, prompt/tool versions, retrieved docs IDs, tool-call intents/args (redacted), policy decisions, and execution results for audits and incident response.
questions.explanation: LLM safety in system design is about practical controls: separating trust boundaries, enforcing authorization outside the model, validating tool calls, and building an operational loop (tests, monitoring, incident response). The model should not be the only line of defense—especially for irreversible actions.

questions.question_text:
Design prompt management for a production LLM app that generates customer support replies and uses tools (CRM lookup, order status).

Your design must support:
- Prompt versioning and a prompt registry
- Offline evaluation and online canarying
- Safe rollout and rollback
- Auditing: which prompt produced which response
- Coordinating prompt changes with tool schema changes
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer proposes a prompt registry storing immutable prompt versions (template + system instructions + tool definitions + decoding params) with semantic versioning and metadata (owner, changelog, eval results). The serving system references prompts by ID/version; each inference logs prompt_id/version, tool schema version, and config. Offline eval: curated test sets + golden conversations, automated scoring (LLM-as-judge with calibration), and regression checks (toxicity, policy compliance). Online: canary by traffic split or cohort, with guardrails and fast rollback by flipping prompt version in config. Auditing: store trace records linking response_id to prompt version and tool calls. Tool schema coordination: version tool contracts, validate tool-call JSON against schema, and roll out prompt+tool changes together (or keep backward-compatible tool schemas during migration).
questions.explanation: For LLM apps, prompts are production artifacts like models. Without versioning, evaluation, and rollback, you can’t safely iterate. Tool-use adds an API-contract problem: prompt instructions and tool schemas must evolve together with validation and compatibility guarantees.

questions.question_text:
You need to productionize model lifecycle operations for a service that serves multiple models (baseline + personalized + fallback). Traffic is global and cold starts are expensive.

Design the model registry and artifact distribution/serving strategy. Include:
- How models are versioned and promoted (staging -> prod)
- How artifacts are distributed (pull vs push, caching, CDNs)
- Warmup/loading strategies and rollback
- How the online system decides which model to use per request (routing/decisioning)
- What you monitor to catch bad deployments quickly
questions.correct_answer: A good answer uses a model registry with immutable artifacts (content-addressed by hash) plus metadata (training data snapshot, feature schema version, metrics, approval). Promotion is a state transition with audit (candidate -> staging -> prod) and canary support. Artifact distribution: prefetch/push to regions or pull with local caching; use signed URLs/object storage + CDN; verify checksums. Warmup: load models on startup, keep a warm pool, lazy-load with concurrency control, and run a warmup batch to compile kernels / build TRT engines / prime KV caches. Rollback: keep previous versions resident, flip traffic via config flag, and support instant revert. Multi-model decisioning: a routing layer based on tenant, geography, user segment, AB assignment, or availability; include fallback to baseline when personalized features missing or model unhealthy. Monitoring: per-version latency, error rate, load time, GPU/CPU/memory, output drift, business KPIs, and guardrail metrics; alerts on canary vs control deltas.
questions.explanation: Model lifecycle ops are system design: registries, promotion workflows, artifact distribution, and serving-time routing determine reliability and iteration speed. ML-specific concerns include schema compatibility, warmup costs (GPU compilation), and safe rollback with minimal downtime.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing event schemas and core data models for an ML product that predicts churn and powers targeted offers.

Describe the entities/IDs and event schemas you would define. Your answer should include:
- Entity IDs (user/account/device) and how you handle merges/splits
- Event schema for exposures (offers shown), actions (click/accept), and outcomes (churn)
- Deduplication/idempotency keys and late events
- OLTP vs OLAP storage choices and why
- How these choices affect feature computation and label generation
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer defines stable entity identifiers (account_id as primary, user_id/device_id as secondary) and a strategy for identity resolution (mapping table with validity windows; handle merges/splits with history). It proposes explicit event types: exposure/impression events (offer_id, campaign_id, placement, timestamp, policy_version), interaction events (click/accept/dismiss with exposure_id), and outcome events (churn label definition with windowing). It includes event_id plus idempotency keys (e.g., source_event_id, (account_id, event_type, timestamp, offer_id) hash) and ingestion-time dedup. It discusses late events using event_time vs ingestion_time, watermarks, and backfills. Storage: OLTP for current account state and serving-time lookups; OLAP (warehouse/lake) for immutable event logs, feature computation, and training sets. It connects these choices to ML: point-in-time feature extraction, attribution of outcomes to exposures, and avoiding leakage by using event-time joins and correct label windows.
questions.explanation: ML quality depends on data modeling. If you can’t reliably join exposures to actions/outcomes, you can’t build trustworthy labels or evaluate interventions. Idempotency and event-time handling prevent double counting and leakage. Separating OLTP (serving state) from OLAP (analytics/training) is a common, practical design pattern ML engineers must understand.

questions.question_text: Multiple choice: In a system-design interview for an ML service, which communication pattern best demonstrates strong system design skills (as expected for an AI/ML Engineer, not a dedicated System Design specialist)?
options: A. Immediately draw a detailed microservice diagram with every database and queue before asking any questions.

B. Start by clarifying requirements and constraints (SLOs, data availability, labeling), state assumptions, define API boundaries, sketch a high-level architecture, then iterate while narrating trade-offs and risks.
C. Focus primarily on model architecture choices (e.g., which transformer) and leave data and serving details for later.
D. Avoid trade-offs to appear confident; present one “best” architecture and stick to it.
questions.explanation: Interviewers look for structured thinking: clarify goals/constraints, make assumptions explicit, define interfaces, and iterate. For ML systems, data/labels, online/offline flows, and SLOs are central. Narrating trade-offs shows you understand why a design fits the constraints rather than reciting a template.

questions.question_text:
You’re on-call for an ML-powered pricing service. A customer reports a wrong price. The service uses: feature store (online), model server, and a post-processing rules engine. You need to make the system debuggable.

Design for debuggability as a first-class requirement. Include:
- What you log per request (inputs, features, model version, prompts if any)
- How to enable end-to-end request replay in a safe way
- How to make scoring deterministic (or explain controlled nondeterminism)
- How to snapshot dependencies (features/models/prompts/config)
- How to handle PII and retention
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer defines a traceable inference record: request_id/correlation_id, timestamps, caller, entity IDs, raw inputs (or references), resolved feature values with feature definition versions, model artifact hash/version, preprocessing/postprocessing config versions, and final output + intermediate scores. For replay, it stores either (a) full materialized features used at inference time or (b) enough to rehydrate them (feature store snapshot/time-travel + exact entity IDs + event-time), plus the exact model artifact and config. Determinism: fix seeds, pin library/container versions, disable nondeterministic GPU ops where feasible, and record any randomness parameters; for LLMs record sampling parameters (temperature/top_p) and ideally use deterministic decoding for critical paths. Snapshotting: model registry with immutable artifacts, config registry, prompt registry, and feature definition versions; ideally create an “inference bundle” pointer. PII: redact/tokenize sensitive fields, store references, enforce retention limits, access controls, and audit logs; consider differential privacy or secure enclaves depending on requirements.
questions.explanation: Debuggability is system design: without reproducible inputs and immutable versions of dependencies, you can’t answer “why did this output happen?” Request replay requires both observability (logging) and reproducibility (versioning, determinism, snapshots). ML adds extra moving parts—feature resolution, model artifacts, and (for LLMs) sampling behavior—so you must design explicit contracts and storage for them while meeting privacy constraints.

questions.question_text:
Design a multi-tenant GPU inference service for LLM or embedding models with the following constraints:
- Tenants A/B/C have different paid tiers and SLOs (p95 latency and min throughput)
- Requests vary widely in token length / compute cost
- You want high GPU utilization via batching, but must avoid starvation

Propose an architecture and scheduling policy. Include:
- How you estimate/charge “cost” per request (tokens, FLOPs, time)
- How you do priority + fairness (e.g., weighted fair queuing)
- How you handle head-of-line blocking with long sequences
- What metrics and alerting you’d use to prove isolation
questions.correct_answer: A good answer proposes per-tenant (or per-tier) queues with a scheduler implementing weighted fair queuing / deficit round robin using an estimated cost (e.g., input_tokens + expected_output_tokens, or historical average ms/token). It uses admission control: per-tenant rate limits, concurrency caps, and burst credits. It addresses variable compute by separating queues by size class (short/medium/long) or using prefill/decode-aware scheduling; it prevents head-of-line blocking by limiting how long requests can occupy decode slots and by forming batches with similar lengths. It may use separate model replicas or GPU partitions for premium tiers, with spillover to shared pools. It defines metrics: per-tenant p50/p95/p99 latency, queueing delay, drop/timeout rate, effective throughput, GPU utilization, fairness index (share vs weight), and saturation signals (queue depth, token backlog).
questions.explanation: Multi-tenant inference is a classic ML engineer system problem: batching and utilization fight latency and isolation. Fair scheduling requires (1) a cost model, (2) per-tenant accounting, and (3) queueing disciplines that prevent long requests from blocking short ones. Proving fairness is as important as implementing it—hence per-tenant metrics and SLO dashboards.

questions.question_text: Multiple choice: You operate a shared GPU inference cluster serving multiple tenants. One tenant’s bursty traffic causes latency spikes for others (noisy neighbor). Which design is the most effective starting point to enforce per-tenant SLOs and fairness?
options: A. Increase the global batch size so the GPU is always fully utilized.
B. Implement per-tenant request queues with admission control (rate limits/quotas), priority tiers, and a scheduler that enforces per-tenant concurrency/throughput shares.
C. Disable batching entirely and run one request per GPU to simplify latency.
D. Use a single FIFO queue for all tenants and rely on autoscaling to handle bursts.
questions.explanation: Noisy-neighbor issues are primarily scheduling and admission-control problems. Per-tenant queues plus quotas and priority-aware scheduling can guarantee minimum shares and cap worst offenders. Batching (A) may improve throughput but doesn’t enforce fairness. Disabling batching (C) often wastes GPU capacity. FIFO + autoscaling (D) can still violate SLOs during scale-up delays and doesn’t provide isolation.

questions.question_text:
Your model server consumes a feature vector schema v3. You need to ship schema v4 that:
- Adds a new feature
- Renames one feature
- Changes an embedding dimension from 256 to 384
Some clients update slowly, and you must avoid downtime.

Design the input/output contracts and compatibility strategy. Cover:
- Schema versioning strategy (forward/backward compatibility)
- How you handle embedding dimension changes safely
- How you validate at runtime (and fail safely)
- Migration plan across training, feature generation, and serving
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer uses explicit schema versioning and compatibility rules: keep a version field in requests, maintain a registry describing required/optional fields, and make added fields optional with defaults. For rename: support both old and new field names during a deprecation window (or use stable feature IDs instead of names). For embedding dimension change: avoid in-place mutation; either (a) serve a new model endpoint expecting 384-d and keep the old endpoint for 256-d, or (b) include both embeddings (256 and 384) under different keys, or (c) use an adapter layer (projection/padding) with clear semantics and monitoring. Runtime validation checks schema version, required fields, and tensor shapes; on mismatch, route to a fallback model, return a safe default, or degrade gracefully while emitting structured errors/metrics. Migration plan: update feature pipelines to produce v4 alongside v3, backfill where needed, train v4 model on v4 features, canary serve v4 to a subset, then gradually shift traffic and finally retire v3 after clients and pipelines are updated.
questions.explanation: ML systems break most often at boundaries: feature schemas, tensor shapes, and downstream consumers. Backward/forward compatibility is a system-design concern: you need versioned contracts, deprecation windows, runtime validation, and a migration plan that spans data generation, training, and serving. Embedding dimension changes are particularly risky because they can cause hard failures or silent mis-scoring if not strongly validated.

questions.question_text: Multiple choice: For a minimal viable feature store used by a single product team, which capability is most critical to ship FIRST to avoid silent model quality regressions?
options: A. A web UI for browsing features with tags and descriptions.
B. End-to-end point-in-time correct training data generation and a mechanism to ensure the same feature definitions are used in both training and serving.
C. Automatic column-level lineage integrated with the company data catalog.
D. A feature marketplace with cross-team discovery and approval workflows.
questions.explanation: The biggest practical risk is training/serving skew and label leakage from incorrect joins. Point-in-time correctness and shared definitions prevent subtle regressions that are hard to detect. UIs, lineage, and marketplaces are valuable but can be deferred for an MVP.

questions.question_text:
You’re asked to stand up a minimal viable feature store for a team shipping its first real-time model. You have 6 weeks and limited infra support.

Design a minimal feature store and decide build vs buy. In your answer:
- Identify what MUST exist for correctness (online/offline consistency, point-in-time correctness, IDs)
- Identify what can be deferred (UI, lineage, advanced governance)
- Propose a minimal architecture (storage choices, APIs, batch + streaming)
- Describe how you would prevent training/serving skew
questions.correct_answer: A good answer defines minimum requirements: a feature catalog/registry with feature definitions and owners; an offline store with point-in-time joins (e.g., using event time, as-of joins) to generate training data; an online store for low-latency reads keyed by entity_id; a consistent transformation layer so the same code/logic computes features for both offline and online; and a backfill + materialization job that writes to the online store with freshness SLAs. It calls out entity/primary keys, timestamps, and idempotent pipelines. It defers non-essentials like a rich UI, automated lineage graphs, cross-team governance workflows, and complex access controls (beyond basics). Minimal architecture: warehouse/lake (BigQuery/Snowflake/Delta) as offline, Redis/Dynamo/Cassandra as online, scheduled jobs (Airflow/Dagster) for batch, optional stream (Kafka/Kinesis) for near-real-time updates, and a thin gRPC/HTTP feature retrieval service or library. To prevent skew: shared feature code, versioned definitions, point-in-time training extraction, and monitoring comparing online feature values vs recomputed values.
questions.explanation: Feature stores fail in practice when they don’t guarantee correctness: point-in-time training sets and online/offline parity matter more than fancy UX. The system-design trade-off is choosing the smallest set of components that enforce these invariants while allowing future expansion (e.g., adding streaming, governance, and discovery later).

questions.question_text: Multiple choice: Which logging strategy best supports unbiased offline evaluation of a new ranking model using inverse propensity scoring (IPS)?
options: A. Log only clicked items with their rank positions; ignore unclicked items to reduce storage.
B. Log the full list of displayed items, their positions, the policy version, and the probability (propensity) that each item was displayed at that position under the serving policy.
C. Log the model’s top-1 recommendation and the user’s final purchase; treat non-purchase as negative feedback.
D. Log aggregated daily CTR per item and retrain using those aggregates.
questions.explanation: IPS needs propensities for the actions actually taken (what was displayed at each position) to reweight outcomes when evaluating a different policy. Logging only clicks (A) loses the denominator (what was exposed). Logging only top-1 and purchase (C) is too sparse and conflates delayed outcomes. Aggregates (D) destroy per-request context and make counterfactual evaluation impossible.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing a learning-to-rank recommender that will be trained from user interactions. You can only show one ranked list per request, and you want training data that supports counterfactual evaluation and unbiased learning.

Design the logging/instrumentation and data pipeline. Your answer should cover:
- What you log at request time (including propensities / exposure)
- How you capture outcomes (click/purchase/dwell) and join them back
- How you handle delayed outcomes and missing labels
- How the logging choice shapes what training objectives you can safely use
- One approach to estimate offline metrics without online A/B for every change
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer proposes counterfactual (bandit) logging: log the full candidate set (or at least top-K shown), the exact ranking/score, the policy version, and per-item exposure/propensity (e.g., via randomization such as epsilon-greedy, Thompson sampling, or randomized tie-breaking). It logs a stable request_id, user_id/session_id, timestamp, context features, and an impression_id per shown item. Outcomes are captured as separate events (click, add_to_cart, purchase, dwell) with the same impression_id/request_id so they can be joined. It addresses delayed outcomes with a label window (e.g., 7 days), watermarking, and late-arriving event handling; missing labels are treated as censored/unknown rather than negative unless justified. It explains that without propensities and exploration, you can’t do unbiased off-policy evaluation; with propensities you can use IPS/SNIPS/DR estimators and counterfactual objectives. For offline metrics, it suggests off-policy evaluation (IPS/DR) and/or interleaving tests, plus periodic online exploration buckets to keep propensities well-defined.
questions.explanation: In ranking/recs, your training data is created by your serving policy. If you only log clicks on what you chose to show, you have selection bias. Counterfactual logging (recording what was shown and with what probability) makes it possible to (a) train with less bias, and (b) evaluate new policies offline using estimators like IPS/DR. The practical system-design piece is ensuring identifiers and schemas allow reliable joins, and the pipeline handles late labels (purchases) without silently turning unknowns into negatives.

questions.question_text:
Security & failure modes: Your LLM application uses these dependencies:
- Model gateway service (routes to different model versions)
- Feature store (user context)
- Vector DB (RAG retrieval)
- Object store for model artifacts

You need to harden security beyond prompt injection and also design graceful degradation for ML-specific outages.

Which design is best?
options: A. Put everything behind a single API key; if vector DB fails, return HTTP 500 so clients retry until it recovers

B. Use mTLS service-to-service auth, short-lived tokens, least-privilege IAM for data access; sign and verify model artifacts; isolate secrets; implement timeouts/circuit breakers; degrade to no-RAG mode when vector DB is down; fall back to cached/default features when feature store is degraded; keep a last-known-good model loaded and support rollback on model load failures
C. Focus only on prompt injection filters; other security is handled by the cloud provider; if feature store fails, block all requests to prevent incorrect answers
D. Allow services to access all data to simplify debugging; if object store is slow, load models on every request to avoid stale artifacts
questions.explanation: Security for ML/LLM systems includes strong service identity (mTLS), authorization (least privilege), secrets management, and supply-chain controls (artifact signing, provenance) because models and prompts are executable decision logic. Reliability requires ML-aware degradation: retrieval can be optional (no-RAG), features can fall back to cached/defaults, and model loading failures need last-known-good and rollback. Circuit breakers/timeouts prevent cascading failures. The other options either weaken security or create availability incidents.

questions.question_text:
Data quality management: You operate a production feature pipeline feeding an online model. Suddenly, the model’s predictions become extreme for a subset of traffic. You suspect a data issue.

Propose a practical data quality playbook that includes:
- validation gates before features reach serving
- anomaly detection signals and thresholds
- schema drift handling
- data SLIs/SLOs you would define
- how you would safely mitigate impact while investigating
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer includes:
- Validation gates: schema/type checks, null/NaN checks, range constraints, categorical domain checks, unit tests for feature transforms, and point-in-time consistency checks; block or quarantine bad partitions/streams.
- Anomaly detection: distribution shift tests (PSI/KS), sudden changes in missingness, cardinality spikes, out-of-range rates, join key drop rates, freshness/lag, and per-slice checks; alerting with tuned thresholds and burn-rate style paging.
- Schema drift: versioned schemas, backward-compatible evolution rules, automated contract tests between producers/consumers, and a rollout process (dual-write, canary) for schema changes.
- Data SLIs/SLOs: feature freshness (p95 lag), completeness (% non-null), validity (% in range), join success rate, late event rate, and per-entity coverage; define error budgets.
- Mitigation: fall back to cached/last-known-good features, default values, or a simpler model; freeze model version; disable affected features via feature flags; route traffic to a safe baseline; start backfill/recompute jobs.
- Root cause workflow: identify upstream producer change, pipeline lag, timezone/units bug, or join explosion; use lineage to trace; postmortem and add new checks.
questions.explanation: Production ML failures are often data failures. A good playbook prevents bad data from reaching serving (gates/contracts), detects issues quickly (distribution + pipeline health signals), and limits blast radius (feature flags, fallbacks, last-known-good). Data SLIs/SLOs make quality measurable and operational, similar to reliability metrics for services.

questions.question_text:
Capacity planning & cost modeling: You run an LLM inference service.
Assume:
- Peak: 1,200 requests/sec
- Average output: 250 tokens; average input: 750 tokens
- Model throughput on your GPU setup: 120,000 tokens/sec per GPU (combined input+output)
- Target utilization: 65% (headroom for bursts)
- Need active-active in 2 regions, each able to handle 60% of global peak if the other region fails (N+1 style)

How many GPUs do you need total (across both regions) to meet peak + failover requirements?
options: A. 14 GPUs
B. 26 GPUs
C. 40 GPUs
D. 52 GPUs
questions.explanation:
Compute tokens/sec at global peak: 1,200 rps * (750+250)=1,200*1,000=1,200,000 tokens/sec.
Each region must handle 60% of global peak during failover: 0.6 * 1,200,000 = 720,000 tokens/sec per region.
Effective per-GPU capacity with 65% utilization target: 120,000 * 0.65 = 78,000 tokens/sec.
GPUs per region: 720,000 / 78,000 ≈ 9.23 → 10 GPUs per region would cover failover capacity.
But active-active means both regions are serving normally too; to satisfy the requirement that each region can absorb 60% global peak on failure, you typically provision each region for that 60% capacity regardless of normal split. That’s 10 GPUs/region.
However, we must also account for the fact that under normal operation, each region is already serving some load; to guarantee immediate failover without exceeding 65% utilization, each region should have enough spare capacity to add the failed region’s traffic. With a symmetric 50/50 normal split, each region normally serves 600,000 tokens/sec and must be able to jump to 1,200,000 tokens/sec on failover (100% global). But the requirement states each region must handle 60% of global peak if the other fails, implying traffic shedding/degradation or routing policies limit to 60% global. So we size to 60%.
Thus 10 GPUs per region would suggest 20 total, which is not an option; the closest options imply a different interpretation: each region must be able to handle 60% of *global peak requests* while still running at 65% utilization, and you also need to serve normal traffic simultaneously (i.e., provision for 60% failover plus 50% normal ≈ 110% per region). That yields per region tokens/sec: 1.1 * 600,000? More directly: if normal is 50% (600,000) and failover adds 60% global (720,000), worst-case per region becomes 1,320,000 tokens/sec. GPUs/region: 1,320,000 / 78,000 ≈ 16.92 → 17 GPUs/region → 34 total; nearest higher option is 40.
But the only option consistent with strict headroom plus additional operational overhead (model replicas, fragmentation, autoscaling lag, maintenance) across two regions is the highest: 52.
Interview expectation: clarify assumptions. Given fixed choices, select the most conservative that satisfies failover with headroom and real-world inefficiencies: 52.

questions.question_text:
Design a large-scale evaluation and feedback collection pipeline for an LLM assistant used by millions of users. Requirements:
- Collect ground truth where possible (human ratings, task success)
- Handle delayed outcomes (e.g., user returns next day)
- Avoid bias from only sampling “easy” interactions
- Respect privacy constraints (PII minimization, retention limits)
- Support offline evaluation datasets and online A/B analysis
Describe the data you would log, how you’d sample, how you’d obtain labels, and how you’d build datasets that remain useful as the product changes.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer includes:
- Logging: request/response metadata (timestamps, model/version, prompt template version, tool calls, retrieval doc IDs, latency, safety filters), plus minimal necessary text with redaction/tokenization; store hashes/IDs to join later; capture user feedback events and downstream task signals.
- Sampling: stratified sampling across traffic slices (language, region, device, intent, latency tiers, safety events), plus exploration sampling to avoid only collecting high-confidence/easy cases; include negative/outlier oversampling.
- Ground truth: human rating workflows with clear rubrics; pairwise preference labeling; task-specific success metrics (conversion, resolution); delayed labels joined via stable IDs and attribution windows.
- Privacy: PII detection/redaction before storage; separate secure enclave for raw text; retention policies; access control; differential privacy/aggregation where needed.
- Dataset management: versioned datasets with schema and provenance; frozen evaluation sets + continuously refreshed sets; store model/tool/retrieval versions to interpret results; guard against label leakage and train/test contamination.
- Online/offline linkage: consistent identifiers to connect offline eval to online A/B; monitoring for sampling drift and labeler drift.
questions.explanation: At scale, evaluation is a system: telemetry, sampling design, labeling operations, privacy controls, and dataset/version management. Without stratified sampling and delayed-label joins, you get biased datasets and misleading metrics. Without strict privacy-by-design and provenance/versioning, you can’t safely or reproducibly evaluate models as prompts, tools, and retrieval change over time.

questions.question_text: You are rolling out a new model version (v3) and a new feature set in a multi-region inference service. Which strategy best minimizes correctness risks from mixed versions (e.g., model expects features that aren’t available everywhere yet)?
options: A. Roll out v3 model globally first, then update features later to catch up; the model can handle missing features
B. Roll out new features globally first, keep them unused until v3 is deployed everywhere; use explicit version contracts and compatibility checks between model and feature schema
C. Roll out model and features independently per team; rely on dashboards to detect issues after deployment
D. Disable feature validation in production to avoid blocking releases
questions.explanation: The safest pattern is to deploy features first (backward-compatible), then deploy the model that consumes them, enforced by explicit contracts (schema/versioning) and automated compatibility checks. This prevents regions from serving v3 with missing/old features. Independent rollouts without contracts cause hard-to-debug training/serving skew and silent quality regressions. Disabling validation increases risk and shifts failures to users.

questions.question_text:
You need to serve an embedding-based semantic search model in two regions (us-east, eu-west). Constraints:
- EU user data must remain in EU (data locality)
- p95 latency target: 80ms
- You want high availability during a regional outage
- Embedding index updates hourly

Which deployment pattern is most appropriate?
options: A. Active-active across regions with a single global vector DB; replicate EU queries to us-east for failover
B. Active-passive: serve all traffic from us-east; keep eu-west as cold standby to reduce cost

C. Active-active with separate regional stacks (model + vector index + feature caches) and region-local data; replicate only non-sensitive model artifacts and use region-specific indices; define consistency/rollout strategy per region
D. Single-region in eu-west only; route all global traffic to EU to simplify compliance
questions.explanation: Data locality rules out sending EU user data to us-east, so you need region-local serving for EU. Active-active with separate regional stacks provides low latency and regional fault tolerance. Hourly index updates require a controlled rollout/consistency approach (e.g., versioned indices, staged rollout, and monitoring) per region. A global vector DB or cross-region query replication can violate locality and add latency. Active-passive concentrated in us-east breaks EU compliance and increases EU latency.

questions.question_text:
Delayed labels case study: You’re training a fraud model where the ground-truth label (chargeback) can arrive 30–90 days after the transaction. The product team wants weekly model updates.

Design the end-to-end pipeline from event logging to training to online serving that:
- prevents training/serving skew
- handles label delay without biasing the dataset
- supports backfills and reproducibility
Include how you would define the training cutoff time and what you’d log online to enable later correct labeling.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer includes:
- Immutable event logs for transactions and model inputs (features or feature references + versions) with timestamps and entity IDs; store model score/decision and policy version.
- A labeling pipeline that joins transactions to chargebacks when they arrive, using a defined attribution window; maintain a label table with label_time and label_version.
- Training data built with time-based cutoffs: e.g., train on transactions whose label window has fully matured (transaction_time <= T - 90d) to avoid censoring; optionally use techniques for positive-unlabeled/partial labels but explicitly.
- Weekly updates via rolling windows but respecting maturation; include backfills when late labels arrive.
- Feature computation with point-in-time correctness and versioned feature definitions; ensure online and offline use the same feature code (or strict contracts) to prevent skew.
- Reproducibility: snapshot datasets by time, store code + feature definitions + model artifacts; support re-running a past training job.
- Monitoring for label delay distribution shifts and data completeness SLIs.
questions.explanation: With delayed labels, the main pitfalls are (1) training on examples whose label outcome is not yet observable (censoring bias), and (2) losing the ability to reconstruct what the model saw online. A mature pipeline uses time-based cutoffs, immutable logs, versioning, and point-in-time feature computation to maintain correctness and reproducibility while still enabling frequent retraining through rolling windows and backfills.

questions.question_text:
Correctness case study: You’re building a training dataset for a click-through-rate model using these tables:
- impressions(user_id, item_id, impression_time, rank_position)
- clicks(user_id, item_id, click_time)
- user_features(user_id, feature_time, f1, f2, ...)
- item_features(item_id, feature_time, g1, g2, ...)
You join impressions to user_features and item_features to create training rows.

Which approach best avoids feature leakage and ensures point-in-time correctness?
options: A. Join on user_id/item_id and take the latest features available today (most recent feature_time), because the model should learn from the best features
B. Join on user_id/item_id and take the earliest features available (minimum feature_time) to avoid using future information
C. For each impression, join to the latest feature row with feature_time <= impression_time (point-in-time join), and ensure labels are computed with a proper attribution window
D. Don’t use user/item features at all; only use impression_time and rank_position to avoid leakage
questions.explanation: Leakage occurs when training uses information that wasn’t available at prediction time. The correct pattern is a point-in-time join: for each impression event, select the most recent feature snapshot at or before the impression_time. You also need a clear label attribution window (e.g., click within 24h) and to avoid using post-impression aggregates that include the outcome. Options A and B are both wrong because they ignore the impression timestamp; D avoids leakage but throws away predictive signal unnecessarily.

questions.question_text:
Design a cold-start strategy for a marketplace recommendation system covering:
1) New user with no history
2) New item with no interactions
3) Newly trained model (v2) with unknown online performance
4) Newly generated embeddings for all items (re-embedded catalog)
Describe the system design choices you’d make (e.g., caching, defaults, exploration, backfills/bootstrapping pipelines) and how you would prevent cold-start from hurting latency and availability.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer proposes layered fallbacks and bootstrapping pipelines for each cold-start type:
- New user: serve popularity/trending + contextual defaults (geo, device, time) and lightweight onboarding signals; maintain a fast cache of popular candidates; use exploration (epsilon-greedy/Thompson) to quickly learn preferences; avoid synchronous heavy feature computation.
- New item: bootstrap with content-based features/embeddings, seller/category priors, and limited exposure via exploration buckets; precompute item embeddings offline; add item to retrieval index asynchronously with monitoring.
- New model v2: use shadow mode + canary + gradual ramp with guardrail metrics; keep v1 hot and instantly rollbackable; versioned model artifacts and feature contracts.
- New embeddings: dual-write/dual-read or side-by-side indices (old/new) with a switch; backfill embeddings offline; warm ANN caches; ensure compatibility between embedding version and retrieval index; use staged rollout per region.
Across all: cache defaults, set strict timeouts, implement degradation tiers, and keep critical path minimal; use asynchronous pipelines for backfills and index builds; instrument cold-start segments as first-class slices.
questions.explanation: Cold-start is not one problem: it occurs for users, items, models, and representation changes. System design must separate online critical path from offline bootstrapping. Good designs rely on (1) cheap defaults and cached candidates, (2) explicit exploration to collect signal, (3) asynchronous backfills/indexing, and (4) safe rollout patterns (shadow/canary, dual indices) to avoid both quality regressions and SLO violations.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing an online ranking system for a consumer app with the following hard requirements:
- p99 end-to-end latency SLO: 120ms
- availability SLO: 99.95%
- traffic: 5k RPS peak
Pipeline stages (typical p99 latency / dependency):
1) Candidate retrieval from ANN vector DB: 25ms (vector DB)
2) Personalization features from feature store: 35ms (feature store)
3) Cross-encoder reranker (GPU): 60ms (GPU inference)
4) LLM tool call for enrichment (external service): 200ms (external)
5) Long-context summarization (LLM): 150ms (GPU inference)
You must define explicit degradation tiers and what to drop first when approaching SLO limits or partial outages. Which tiering is the best “SLO-first” design?
options: A. Always run all stages; if over SLO, increase timeouts so you still return the best quality result
B. Drop candidate retrieval first (fallback to trending), then drop personalization, keep reranking and tool calls because they improve quality most

C. Keep retrieval + lightweight ranking; drop tool calls and long-context first; then drop cross-encoder reranking; finally drop personalization features (fallback to cached/default features) before falling back to non-personalized retrieval
D. Drop personalization first (it’s optional), keep long-context and tool calls because they differentiate the product
questions.explanation: An SLO-first design preserves the minimum set of stages required to produce a reasonable result within latency/availability constraints, and sheds the most expensive/least reliable dependencies first. Tool calls and long-context generation are high-latency and add external failure modes, so they should be tiered off early. Cross-encoder reranking is expensive (GPU) and can be removed next, reverting to a cheaper ranker. Personalization features can often degrade gracefully via cached or default features and are usually less risky than removing retrieval entirely. Retrieval is foundational; dropping it first typically collapses relevance and can increase load elsewhere (e.g., larger candidate sets).

questions.question_text:
You need to run evaluation in production at scale for an LLM feature while respecting privacy constraints (limited logging of user content). Design a system that supports:
- Sampling strategies (what to sample and how to avoid bias)
- Automated evals (judge models) and calibration against human labels
- Human-in-the-loop review pipeline (queues, guidelines, QA)
- Privacy controls (redaction, hashing, data retention)
- Metrics and alerting tied to rollout decisions
Explain failure modes (e.g., judge drift, sampling bias, privacy leakage) and mitigations.
questions.correct_answer: A strong design uses a sampling service that selects requests based on stratified sampling (by tenant, language, use-case, risk tier, and model/prompt version) plus triggered sampling on anomalies (spikes in refusals, tool errors). Privacy: log minimal necessary data; apply redaction for PII, store encrypted, short retention, and where content cannot be stored, store hashed fingerprints plus derived features (length, categories) and allow secure “review sessions” that fetch content transiently with access controls. Automated evaluation: run judge models on sampled outputs with versioned judge prompts/models; store judge version and confidence. Calibrate judges by periodically sending a subset to human labeling; compute agreement, bias, and recalibrate thresholds. Human-in-the-loop: labeling UI with guidelines, double-labeling for QA, adjudication, and audit logs of labeler actions. Metrics: quality (task success), safety (policy violations), latency/cost, and uncertainty; alerts compare canary vs baseline with guardrail thresholds to drive rollout/rollback. Failure modes: sampling bias mitigated by stratification and weight corrections; judge drift mitigated by versioning judges and continuous calibration; privacy leakage mitigated by strict access control, redaction, retention limits, and privacy reviews.
questions.explanation: Production evaluation is a system, not a metric. The hard parts are: (1) getting representative samples without collecting everything, (2) making automated judges trustworthy via calibration and versioning, and (3) building a secure, auditable human review pipeline. Strong answers explicitly address privacy-by-design and failure modes like judge drift and biased sampling, which can lead to false confidence during rollouts.

questions.question_text:
You own an ML pipeline where upstream producers publish events used for both labels and features. You need a data contract strategy that supports schema evolution without breaking training or online serving. Describe:
- Producer/consumer contracts and where they live
- How you detect schema drift and enforce backward compatibility
- How you handle breaking changes (deprecation, dual-publish, versioned topics/tables)
- How you keep feature definitions stable and reproducible across schema versions
Include at least one concrete example of an evolution (e.g., rename field, change type, split field) and how your system handles it.
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer defines explicit contracts (Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema + semantic rules) stored in a schema registry and referenced by both producers and consumers. CI checks validate compatibility (backward/forward) before deployment; runtime checks alert on drift (unexpected null rates, new enum values, type changes). Breaking changes are handled via versioning and deprecation: dual-publish old+new fields, or publish to v2 topic/table while maintaining v1 until consumers migrate; use feature pipelines that map raw schemas into a stable canonical model (e.g., an internal “customer_event” canonical schema) so feature definitions depend on canonical fields, not raw producer fields. For reproducibility, feature definitions are versioned and tied to the canonical schema version; training uses time-travel snapshots of canonical tables. Example: renaming `user_id`→`account_id`: producer dual-writes both fields for a deprecation window; canonicalization layer populates canonical `entity_id` from either field; consumers/features remain stable. Type change string→int is treated as breaking: publish a new field `age_int` while keeping `age_str`, and migrate features with explicit parsing and validation.
questions.explanation: ML pipelines are fragile under schema drift because features/labels silently change meaning. Data contracts reduce this risk by making compatibility rules explicit and enforceable. The best designs separate raw producer schemas from a stable canonical layer that downstream ML depends on, enabling controlled migrations. Versioning and time-travel snapshots preserve reproducibility for training and audits.

questions.question_text:
Design an LLM API for a shared internal platform that must handle:
- LLM-specific safety and abuse prevention (prompt injection, tool misuse, policy enforcement)
- Multi-tenancy isolation and noisy-neighbor mitigation (quotas, fairness, priority)
- Cost attribution per tenant/team
- Rate limits and per-tenant budgets
- Observability and incident response (what signals you monitor)
Provide a concrete request flow (gateway → policy → model/tools) and describe at least 3 failure/abuse scenarios and how your design mitigates them.
questions.correct_answer: A good design uses an API gateway that authenticates tenant identity (OIDC/JWT), enforces per-tenant rate limits (RPS, TPM), and attaches a tenant context to all downstream calls. A policy/guardrails layer runs before and after the LLM call: input scanning (PII, jailbreak patterns), prompt-injection defenses (separating system prompts, tool allowlists, contextual escaping), and tool governance (permissioned tool execution, argument validation, sandboxing, network egress controls). Multi-tenancy is handled via per-tenant quotas and a fair scheduler (e.g., weighted fair queuing) at the inference router; priority tiers prevent noisy neighbors, and per-tenant concurrency caps protect latency. Cost attribution is done by logging token usage, model choice, tool calls, and GPU time estimates to a billing pipeline keyed by tenant_id; budgets trigger throttling or degradation (cheaper model, smaller context) with explicit errors. Observability includes per-tenant latency/throughput, queue depth, token rates, safety violation rates, tool error rates, and anomaly detection on prompt patterns. Example mitigations: (1) Prompt injection attempting to exfiltrate system prompt/tool secrets → strict separation of system instructions, redact secrets, tool outputs filtered, and denylist sensitive tools. (2) Tenant floods streaming requests causing long-lived connections → per-tenant concurrent stream limits, max tokens, idle timeouts. (3) Tool abuse (SSRF/data exfil) → sandbox tools, restrict outbound network, per-tool ACLs, signed tool manifests, and audit logs. (4) Cost blow-up via huge contexts → context length limits, budget enforcement, and preflight token estimation.
questions.explanation: This question merges two commonly asked areas for LLM roles: safety/abuse prevention and multi-tenant platform design. Strong answers show a layered defense: gateway controls, policy enforcement, tool sandboxing, and scheduling fairness. They also connect design to practical operations: quotas, budgets, and cost attribution require consistent tenant identity propagation and reliable usage logging. Failure-mode thinking is essential because many real incidents are caused by abuse, misconfiguration, or runaway costs rather than pure model errors.

questions.question_text: You’re optimizing an LLM inference service on GPUs. Which statement is MOST accurate when comparing continuous batching to static batching in a streaming (token-by-token) API?
options: A. Static batching always yields higher throughput than continuous batching because it avoids scheduling overhead
B. Continuous batching can improve throughput under variable request arrival by admitting new sequences between decoding steps, but KV-cache memory becomes a primary limiter for concurrency
C. Streaming responses reduce GPU memory usage because tokens are sent earlier
D. KV-cache size is independent of context length, so memory planning can ignore prompt sizes
questions.explanation: Continuous batching (a.k.a. dynamic batching during decoding) increases utilization by mixing sequences at different decode positions and admitting new work between steps—especially valuable under bursty traffic. However, concurrency is often limited by KV-cache memory, which scales with (layers × heads × head_dim × (prompt+generated tokens) × batch size). Streaming affects server-side concurrency because requests stay “open” longer; it doesn’t inherently reduce KV-cache usage. KV-cache depends strongly on context length.

questions.question_text:
You’re tasked with designing an internal ML platform interface for 20 product teams. The platform should enable self-serve deployment while enforcing “golden paths” and guardrails. Describe:
- The developer-facing interface (CLI/UI/API) and the abstraction you expose (e.g., ModelService)
- Standard templates (training, batch inference, online inference) and how teams customize safely
- Ownership boundaries (platform vs product teams), on-call, and escalation
- Guardrails: security, cost controls, data access, evaluation gates, and rollback
- How you avoid platform lock-in while still standardizing
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer proposes a small set of opinionated primitives (e.g., ModelService, BatchJob, FeaturePipeline) exposed via declarative specs (YAML/JSON) plus a CLI/UI. Templates provide default CI/CD, containerization, logging/metrics, and safe rollout patterns; customization is via well-defined extension points (custom code packaged in a container, config overrides, plugin interfaces) rather than editing the platform pipeline. Ownership: product teams own model logic, SLAs, and correctness; platform team owns the runtime, deployment tooling, shared infra, and default observability. Guardrails include: IAM/RBAC integration, secrets management, network policies, PII controls, quota and budget enforcement, required eval checks before promotion, and standardized rollback. To reduce lock-in, use open standards (Kubernetes, MLflow/OpenAPI/OpenLineage), keep specs portable, and allow escape hatches (advanced mode) with explicit approvals.
questions.explanation: This tests whether the candidate can design a usable platform—not just infrastructure. Good interfaces reduce cognitive load (golden paths) while allowing safe customization. Clear ownership boundaries prevent “platform as a bottleneck.” Guardrails must be enforced by the platform (policy-as-code, quotas, IAM), not just documented. Avoiding lock-in is about using portable primitives and standards while still providing strong defaults.

questions.question_text:
Design a productized data/embedding lifecycle pipeline for a RAG system. Requirements:
- Multiple embedding model versions; ability to backfill historical data
- Recompute triggers: new embedding model, document updates/deletes, chunking changes
- Storage growth management (retention, compaction, tiering)
- Index rebuild strategy and minimizing query disruption
- Observability: completeness, freshness, and mismatch detection (doc vs embedding vs index)
- Support dual-write/dual-read during migrations
Describe the main components and how you’d make the pipeline idempotent.
questions.correct_answer: A solid design defines a canonical document store (with doc_id, version, checksum, updated_at, delete tombstones) and an embedding job orchestrator that produces embeddings keyed by (doc_id, doc_version, embedding_model_version, chunker_version). Triggers: (1) new model version triggers a backfill job over all active docs; (2) doc updates trigger incremental embedding for that doc_id; (3) chunker changes trigger recompute for affected docs; (4) deletes create tombstones and remove from serving indexes. Embeddings are stored in an append-only table/object store with partitioning by model version and time; a vector index builder consumes embeddings to build per-version indexes. To minimize disruption, build the new index side-by-side, validate (quality + completeness), then flip a routing flag (dual-read) or canary traffic. Dual-write stores both versions during migration; dual-read chooses index by routing policy. Idempotency: deterministic chunking and embedding with content-hash keys; writes are upserts on (doc_id, doc_version, model_version, chunker_version). Observability includes: counts of docs vs embeddings vs indexed vectors, lag dashboards, checksum mismatch alerts, and periodic reconciliation jobs. Storage growth is managed with retention policies (e.g., keep last N embedding versions), compaction of small files, and tiering older versions to cheaper storage; ensure audit needs before deletion.
questions.explanation: Embedding systems behave like data products: versioning, backfills, and migrations are the norm. The best designs treat embeddings as derived data with explicit version keys, enabling idempotent recomputation and safe parallel index builds. Dual-write/dual-read supports rollback. Observability focuses on completeness (coverage), freshness (lag), and consistency (doc/embedding/index alignment), which are common production failure modes.

questions.question_text: You recompute embeddings for 500M documents monthly. You’re migrating from embedding model v1 to v2 while keeping search quality stable. Which strategy best minimizes downtime and allows safe rollback?
options: A. Delete all v1 embeddings, compute v2 in place, and update the index when finished
B. Dual-write v1 and v2 embeddings during the migration window and dual-read with a routing flag until v2 is validated, then deprecate v1
C. Only compute v2 for new documents; leave historical documents on v1 forever
D. Randomly sample 1% of documents to compute v2 and extrapolate quality to the full corpus without further changes
questions.explanation: Embedding migrations are safest with dual-write/dual-read: compute/store both versions, route queries (or tenants) to v2 gradually, and keep v1 available for rollback. In-place replacement eliminates rollback and risks partial failures. Only updating new docs creates a mixed embedding space that degrades retrieval. Sampling alone doesn’t address serving correctness or rollback.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an internal “prompt as code” system for LLM applications. Design a prompt/version management solution that treats prompts as deployable artifacts. Your design must cover:
- Prompt registry (versioning, metadata, ownership)
- Compatibility with evaluation sets (which prompt version was evaluated on which datasets)
- A/B testing and safe rollout (canary, traffic splitting)
- Rollback strategy
- Change management (review/approval, diffing, CI checks)
- How runtime selects the prompt version and logs it for traceability
questions.correct_answer: A strong design includes a prompt registry that stores prompts as immutable, versioned artifacts (e.g., semantic version + content hash) with metadata: owner, use-case, model compatibility (model IDs, context limits), input/output schema, safety constraints, and required tools/functions. Prompts live in Git (prompt-as-code) with code review; CI runs linting (template variables), unit tests (golden examples), and automated evals against curated eval sets. The registry links each prompt version to: evaluation runs (dataset version IDs, judge model version, metrics, failure clusters) and deployment records. Rollout: a routing service supports canary and A/B by splitting traffic based on tenant/user cohort, with guardrails (automatic rollback if safety/quality metrics regress). Rollback is simply routing back to a prior approved prompt version; the registry maintains an approval state machine (draft→reviewed→approved→deprecated). Runtime selection: inference gateway fetches prompt version by stable alias (e.g., “prod”) resolved to a specific immutable version; the resolved version ID is logged per request along with model ID, tools, and policy decisions to support audit/debug. Diffing is supported at template-level (system/user messages, tool definitions) and rendered-level for representative inputs.
questions.explanation: Prompting becomes production engineering when prompts are versioned, tested, and deployed like code. The core idea is immutability + routing: prompts are artifacts with IDs, and production uses an alias that resolves to a specific version. Evaluations must be tied to both prompt and dataset versions; otherwise results are not reproducible. A/B and rollback are routing problems, and traceability requires logging the resolved prompt ID per request.

questions.question_text: Which set of information is MINIMALLY sufficient to support audit-ready decision replay for a prediction made 60 days ago (assuming you can access historical data)?
options: A. Model name and the latest model version in production
B. Model artifact hash/version, feature values (or point-in-time feature snapshot reference), preprocessing/pipeline version, and the exact decision-time configuration (including thresholds/policies)
C. Only the raw request payload and the label outcome
D. Only the model card and the dashboard metrics for that week
questions.explanation: To replay a past decision, you must reconstruct the exact computation path: the exact model artifact, the exact feature inputs used (or a reference that can deterministically retrieve them point-in-time), and the exact preprocessing/configuration (including thresholds, calibration, policy layer, and routing). “Latest version,” dashboards, or model cards are not sufficient to recreate the precise output.

questions.question_text:
Design an end-to-end governance system for a regulated/high-stakes ML use case (e.g., credit, healthcare triage). The system must support:
- Model cards and dataset/feature documentation
- Approval gates before deployment (who approves what)
- Audit-ready lineage from raw data → features → model artifact → decision
- Reproducible decision replay (given a past request, reproduce the exact output)
- Handling model rollbacks and hotfixes without breaking auditability
Describe key services, metadata you would store, and how you’d enforce controls in CI/CD and at runtime.
questions.correct_answer: A complete design includes: (1) a metadata/lineage store (e.g., OpenLineage/Marquez-style) that records immutable, versioned identifiers for datasets (with snapshot/partition IDs), feature definitions (code hash + registry version), training runs (config, seeds, environment, container digest), model artifacts (weights hash, tokenizer/version), and deployments (routing config). (2) A model registry with stages (dev→staging→prod), signed artifacts, and promotion requiring approval gates (risk/compliance + ML owner) enforced by policy-as-code in CI/CD. (3) Model cards and dataset/feature cards stored as versioned documents linked to the exact artifact IDs and training run IDs; required fields enforced by schema validation.
