questions.explanation: Caching is a classic performance fundamental: it can dramatically reduce latency and cost when there is input repetition, but it introduces staleness and invalidation complexity. In ML, invalidation can be triggered by model version changes, feature updates, or changing business rules. Good designs include cache keys that incorporate model/version and feature versions, plus TTLs and explicit invalidation.

questions.question_text: Free response: Describe what idempotency means in the context of an ML inference API and give one practical technique to achieve it.
questions.correct_answer: Idempotency means that making the same request multiple times has the same effect as making it once (no unintended additional side effects). For an ML inference API, repeated requests (due to retries/timeouts) should not create duplicate downstream actions (e.g., duplicate writes, duplicate notifications). A common technique is to require an idempotency key (request ID) and store the result keyed by that ID (or store a processed-request record) so retries return the same response or are safely ignored.
questions.explanation: Retries are fundamental in distributed systems, but they can cause duplicate work. Idempotency ensures safe retries. In ML systems, even if inference itself is “pure,” side effects like logging, metering, writing predictions, or triggering workflows can duplicate. Using an idempotency key plus a dedup store (cache/DB with TTL) is a standard pattern.

questions.question_text: An inference service experiences traffic spikes. Which design fundamental most directly helps protect the service from overload while maintaining predictable behavior?
options: A. Backpressure (e.g., bounded queues, rejecting/deferring requests when saturated)
B. Increasing model size to improve accuracy
C. Using a single shared database for all microservices
D. Disabling timeouts so requests can always finish
questions.explanation: Backpressure is a fundamental resilience technique: when downstream capacity is saturated, you must shed load, queue with bounds, or degrade gracefully. Unbounded queues and disabled timeouts often cause cascading failures (memory blowups, thread exhaustion). Backpressure is especially important for GPU-bound inference where concurrency is limited.

questions.question_text: You need to choose between synchronous and asynchronous processing for an ML workload. Which scenario is the best fit for asynchronous processing?
options: A. Real-time user-facing autocomplete with a 100ms p95 latency target
B. Fraud detection that must block a transaction immediately
C. Generating nightly embeddings for a large catalog where results can arrive minutes later
D. A health-check endpoint used by a load balancer
questions.explanation: Asynchronous processing is ideal when work is long-running and the caller can tolerate delay (e.g., batch embedding generation, offline feature computation). User-facing and blocking decisions (A, B) typically need synchronous responses. Health checks (D) are lightweight and synchronous.

questions.question_text: In a typical AI/ML inference system, what is the most important reason to decouple the request-serving layer from the model execution layer (e.g., separate API gateway/web tier from model workers)?
options: A. It guarantees the model will be 100% accurate
B. It allows independent scaling and failure isolation between traffic handling and compute-heavy inference
C. It eliminates the need for monitoring and logging
D. It makes the system strongly consistent by default
questions.explanation: Decoupling is a core system design fundamental: different tiers have different scaling needs and failure modes. The web tier scales with connections and request parsing; model workers scale with GPU/CPU and memory. Isolation reduces blast radius (e.g., model worker crash doesn’t take down routing) and enables independent autoscaling policies.

questions.question_text: You are designing an online inference endpoint. Which statement best describes the difference between p50 and p99 latency?
options: A. p50 is the fastest possible latency; p99 is the slowest possible latency
B. p50 is the median request latency; p99 is the latency below which 99% of requests complete
C. p50 measures CPU time; p99 measures network time
D. p50 is only relevant for batch jobs; p99 is only relevant for online systems
questions.explanation: Percentiles summarize the distribution of latencies. p50 is the median; p99 captures tail latency (the slowest ~1% of requests). Tail latency is critical in ML services because occasional slow model loads, GC pauses, cold starts, or cache misses can hurt user experience even if median latency looks fine.

questions.question_text: In system design for an AI/ML service, what is the most accurate definition of "throughput"?
options: A. The time it takes for a single request to complete end-to-end
B. The number of requests the system can successfully process per unit time under a given workload
C. The fraction of time the system is available and accepting requests
D. The maximum size of a request payload the system can accept
questions.explanation: Throughput measures capacity over time (e.g., requests/second). Latency is per-request time (A). Availability is uptime (C). Payload size limits are unrelated (D). In ML inference, you often track both throughput (QPS) and latency (p50/p95/p99) because batching can improve throughput while potentially increasing latency.

questions.question_text: Free text: Describe the *fundamental* difference between throughput and latency in an ML inference system, and explain why optimizing only throughput can still lead to poor user experience. Include one example involving batching.
questions.correct_answer: Throughput is how many requests the system can process per unit time (e.g., rps), while latency is how long a single request takes end-to-end (e.g., p95 in ms). Optimizing only throughput can hurt user experience because you might increase queueing or waiting time, worsening tail latency. Example: increasing batch size on a GPU can raise throughput (more items per forward pass) but adds batching wait time (requests wait to fill a batch), which can increase p95/p99 latency beyond the SLO even though overall rps improves.
questions.explanation: The fundamental relationship is that users experience latency, not aggregate throughput. Many ML optimizations (batching, pipelining, async prefetch) trade latency for throughput. Good system design explicitly sets latency SLOs and then tunes batching windows, max batch size, and autoscaling to meet both throughput needs and tail-latency targets.

questions.question_text: Free text: In system design terms, define idempotency and explain why it is a *fundamental* requirement when building an async inference pipeline (queue-based) that retries on failure. Provide one concrete example of an idempotency key.
questions.correct_answer: Idempotency means performing the same operation multiple times has the same effect as performing it once. In an async inference pipeline with retries, the same job/message may be delivered more than once; without idempotency, you can produce duplicate side effects (e.g., double-writing results, double-charging, emitting duplicate events). A common approach is to include an idempotency key such as a stable request_id (e.g., UUID generated by the client) or a deterministic hash of (user_id, input_id, model_version, timestamp_bucket) stored/checked in a results table before writing.
questions.explanation: Retries and at-least-once delivery are fundamental properties of many queue systems. Idempotency is how you make correctness robust to duplicates. Practically, you implement it by writing results with a unique constraint on the idempotency key, using conditional writes (compare-and-set), or checking a dedup store before processing side effects.

questions.question_text: Free text: Explain the *fundamental* difference between horizontal and vertical scaling for an inference service, and give one practical reason an AI/ML team might prefer horizontal scaling even when a larger GPU exists.
questions.correct_answer: Vertical scaling increases the capacity of a single node (e.g., bigger GPU/CPU/memory), while horizontal scaling adds more nodes/replicas behind a load balancer. Teams often prefer horizontal scaling because it improves reliability and elasticity (fail one replica without total outage, scale out for bursts), and it can reduce tail latency by distributing load. It can also simplify deployments (rolling updates) and avoid single-node resource limits or contention.
questions.explanation: The fundamental concept is how capacity is added: “scale up” vs “scale out.” For inference, horizontal scaling commonly pairs with load balancing and autoscaling policies, which helps meet SLOs under variable traffic and reduces blast radius. Vertical scaling can be simpler initially but can hit hard ceilings (max GPU size), and a single large node can become a single point of failure or contention hotspot.

questions.question_text:
In system design, you often discuss data consistency. For an online feature store used by real-time inference, what is the *fundamental* implication of choosing eventual consistency for feature updates?

A) Reads will always return the latest committed value globally
B) Some predictions may use slightly stale features, so you must reason about acceptable staleness bounds
C) The system cannot scale horizontally
D) Write throughput will always be lower than read throughput
options: A) Reads will always return the latest committed value globally
B) Some predictions may use slightly stale features, so you must reason about acceptable staleness bounds
C) The system cannot scale horizontally
D) Write throughput will always be lower than read throughput
questions.explanation: Eventual consistency means replicas converge over time; reads may observe stale data. The fundamental design question becomes: how much staleness is acceptable for prediction quality and fairness, and how do you bound/monitor it (e.g., TTLs, versioning, timestamps, read-your-writes where needed). It’s not inherently unscalable; it’s a trade-off for availability/latency.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing a model deployment pipeline. What is the *fundamental* purpose of a canary release for ML models?

A) Increase training speed by splitting data across clusters
B) Reduce model size by pruning weights in production
C) Limit blast radius by exposing a new model to a small percentage of traffic and monitoring key metrics before full rollout
D) Avoid the need for monitoring by guaranteeing correctness
options: A) Increase training speed by splitting data across clusters
B) Reduce model size by pruning weights in production
C) Limit blast radius by exposing a new model to a small percentage of traffic and monitoring key metrics before full rollout
D) Avoid the need for monitoring by guaranteeing correctness
questions.explanation: A canary release is a fundamental reliability mechanism. It reduces risk by gradually rolling out changes and validating behavior on real traffic. For ML, it helps catch distribution shifts, feature issues, latency regressions, and metric degradation (e.g., CTR, precision/recall proxies) before impacting all users.

questions.question_text:
You need to choose between synchronous (request/response) inference and asynchronous (queue + callback/polling) inference. What is the *fundamental* factor that most strongly drives this choice?

A) Whether you can use Python or must use C++
B) Whether the client can tolerate variable and potentially long processing time
C) Whether the model is a neural network or a tree model
D) Whether the data is stored in SQL or NoSQL
options: A) Whether you can use Python or must use C++
B) Whether the client can tolerate variable and potentially long processing time
C) Whether the model is a neural network or a tree model
D) Whether the data is stored in SQL or NoSQL
questions.explanation: The core decision is driven by latency requirements and user interaction patterns. If the client needs an immediate answer (tight latency SLO), synchronous inference is typical. If work can take longer, can be retried, or can be processed later (e.g., document processing, large LLM jobs), async with a queue improves resilience and smoothing of bursts.

questions.question_text:
In an online feature store, what is the *fundamental* reason engineers emphasize point-in-time correctness for training data generation?

A) It reduces GPU memory usage during training
B) It prevents label leakage by ensuring features are computed using only information available at prediction time
C) It improves model accuracy by always using the newest features
D) It makes batch jobs run faster by avoiding joins
options: A) It reduces GPU memory usage during training
B) It prevents label leakage by ensuring features are computed using only information available at prediction time
C) It improves model accuracy by always using the newest features
D) It makes batch jobs run faster by avoiding joins
questions.explanation: Point-in-time correctness is a fundamental ML system design requirement to prevent training/serving skew and label leakage. If training features inadvertently include future information (not available at inference time), offline metrics look great but production performance collapses. The goal is to reproduce the exact information boundary that exists at prediction time.

questions.question_text:
You’re asked to size an inference service. The model takes ~40 ms GPU time per request, and you expect 250 requests/second steady-state with occasional bursts to 400 rps. Ignoring overheads, what is the *fundamental* back-of-the-envelope minimum GPU concurrency (or equivalent parallelism) needed to sustain 250 rps?

A) 1
B) 5
C) 10
D) 100
questions.explanation: Use Little’s Law style reasoning: throughput ≈ concurrency / service_time ⇒ concurrency ≈ throughput × service_time. For 250 rps and 0.040 s per request: 250 × 0.040 = 10 concurrent in-flight requests. In practice you add headroom for overheads, batching inefficiency, and burst handling, but 10 is the minimum baseline estimate.

questions.question_text:
In a system design interview, you propose caching model predictions. Which statement best reflects the *fundamental* trade-off you must evaluate first?

A) Caching always improves accuracy because it reduces compute
B) Caching trades freshness/correctness for lower latency and cost
C) Caching trades higher latency for lower memory usage
D) Caching is only useful for batch inference, not online inference
options: A) Caching always improves accuracy because it reduces compute
B) Caching trades freshness/correctness for lower latency and cost
C) Caching trades higher latency for lower memory usage
D) Caching is only useful for batch inference, not online inference
questions.explanation: Caching is fundamentally a time-vs-freshness trade-off: you can reduce latency and compute cost by reusing results, but risk serving stale predictions if inputs, features, or the model change. For ML, correctness can degrade due to feature drift, model updates, or time-sensitive features, so cache keys, TTLs, and invalidation strategy are central.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing an online inference service for an ML model. Which metric is the best *fundamental* starting point to define and measure SLOs for end-user experience?

A) Average (mean) latency across all requests
B) p95/p99 latency and error rate (availability)
C) CPU utilization on inference hosts
D) Total number of requests per day
options: A) Average (mean) latency across all requests
B) p95/p99 latency and error rate (availability)
C) CPU utilization on inference hosts
D) Total number of requests per day
questions.explanation: SLOs should reflect user-perceived reliability and performance. Tail latency (p95/p99) captures the slowest experiences that users notice, while error rate captures availability. Mean latency can hide long-tail slow requests, and CPU utilization/request counts are internal signals that don’t directly define user experience.

questions.question_text: Fundamentally, what is "backpressure" in a high-throughput inference pipeline, and how would you implement it at a basic level?
questions.correct_answer: Backpressure is a mechanism that prevents upstream components from overwhelming downstream components when capacity is exceeded. In an inference pipeline, it means slowing, rejecting, or buffering incoming work when model workers, GPUs, or downstream services are saturated. Basic implementations include: limiting concurrent in-flight requests (semaphores), returning 429/503 when queues exceed a threshold, using bounded queues with drop/timeout policies, and applying rate limiting per client. The goal is to keep the system stable (avoid cascading failures and timeouts) while providing predictable degradation under load.
questions.explanation: Backpressure is a core distributed-systems fundamental and shows up constantly in ML inference (GPU saturation, bursty traffic). A solid answer defines it in terms of protecting downstream capacity and lists concrete primitives (concurrency limits, bounded queues, load shedding, rate limiting) and the reason (stability and graceful degradation).

questions.question_text: An inference service calls 5 downstream services (feature store, embedding service, user profile, rules engine, logging). Each has 99.9% availability independently. Assuming independence, what is the approximate end-to-end availability if all 5 must succeed for a request to succeed?
questions.explanation: End-to-end availability for a serial dependency chain is approximately the product: 0.999^5 ≈ 0.995 ≈ 99.5%. This is a fundamental system-design insight: adding dependencies reduces overall reliability. ML inference stacks often have multiple calls; to improve availability you can reduce dependencies, add fallbacks (e.g., cached/default features), or make some calls best-effort (e.g., async logging).

questions.question_text: Which option best describes the fundamental trade-off between consistency and availability during a network partition (CAP) in a feature store used for online inference?
options: A. You can always get both strong consistency and full availability during a partition if you use retries
B. During a partition, a system must choose between serving potentially stale/incorrect data (favor availability) or rejecting/limiting reads/writes to maintain consistency
C. CAP only applies to relational databases, not feature stores
D. Choosing consistency means the system will always have lower latency
questions.explanation: CAP states that in the presence of a network partition, you can’t simultaneously guarantee both strong consistency and availability. A feature store must decide: serve reads that might be stale (AP-leaning) or fail/limit operations to preserve strong consistency (CP-leaning). Retries don’t remove the fundamental trade-off. CAP applies to any distributed data system, and consistency choices don’t automatically imply lower latency.

questions.question_text: You are designing a feature retrieval path for online inference. Fundamentally, why is it risky to compute features using a different code path online vs. offline training?
questions.correct_answer: Using different feature computation paths online vs. offline creates training-serving skew: the model is trained on one feature distribution/definition but served with another, leading to degraded accuracy and unpredictable behavior. Differences can come from mismatched windowing logic, missing data handling, normalization, categorical mapping, or timestamp cutoffs. The fundamental mitigation is to reuse the same feature definitions and transformations (shared libraries/feature store), version features, and validate parity between offline and online computations.
questions.explanation: Training-serving skew is a fundamental ML system-design concern: correctness of the entire system depends on consistent inputs. This question checks whether the candidate understands that system design for ML includes data/feature consistency, not just infrastructure. A good answer names skew, gives concrete sources of mismatch, and proposes fundamental mitigations like shared definitions, versioning, and parity tests.

questions.question_text: In a distributed ML system, what is the fundamental meaning of "idempotency" for an inference request handler?
questions.correct_answer: Idempotency means that processing the same request multiple times has the same effect as processing it once. For an inference handler, this typically means repeated retries (due to timeouts or network errors) should not cause duplicated side effects (e.g., double-writing logs with billing impact, double-updating counters, double-publishing events) and should ideally return the same prediction result for the same inputs and model version. Achieving this often involves request IDs, deduplication, and making side effects conditional or transactional.
questions.explanation: This is a core system-design fundamental because retries are unavoidable in distributed systems. ML engineers often build services with downstream writes (feature logging, analytics events, audit trails). Understanding idempotency ensures correctness under retries and partial failures. A complete answer mentions “same effect,” retries, and controlling side effects via request identifiers/deduplication.

questions.question_text: An online inference service must handle spikes in traffic. Which is the most fundamental reason to use a queue (or buffering) between request intake and model execution in some architectures?
options: A. Queues make the system strongly consistent by default
B. Queues smooth bursty load and provide backpressure, preventing overload of downstream compute
C. Queues always reduce end-to-end latency
D. Queues eliminate the need for autoscaling
questions.explanation: Queues/buffers are a fundamental load-leveling tool: they decouple producers from consumers, absorb bursts, and enable backpressure so downstream workers don’t get overwhelmed. They can increase end-to-end latency (waiting time), so they’re a trade-off. Queues don’t inherently provide strong consistency, and autoscaling may still be needed to drain the queue under sustained load.

questions.question_text: Which statement best captures the fundamental difference between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling for an ML inference service?
options: A. Vertical scaling adds more instances; horizontal scaling adds more CPU to one instance
B. Vertical scaling increases resources of a single node; horizontal scaling adds more nodes and distributes load across them
C. Vertical scaling improves availability; horizontal scaling only improves latency
D. Vertical scaling is always cheaper than horizontal scaling
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling (scale up) means giving a single machine more CPU/RAM/GPU; horizontal scaling (scale out) means adding more machines/replicas and load balancing. Fundamentally, horizontal scaling often improves fault tolerance and capacity but introduces distributed-systems concerns (load balancing, consistency, coordination). Costs and performance depend on workload and infrastructure; neither is always cheaper.

questions.question_text: Fundamentally, why do systems introduce caching in front of an ML feature store or inference service?
questions.correct_answer: Caching is introduced to reduce repeated work and reduce latency by serving frequently requested data/results from a faster layer (memory/edge) instead of recomputing or fetching from a slower backend. In ML systems, caching can (1) lower p95/p99 latency, (2) increase throughput by reducing backend load, and (3) improve cost efficiency by reducing compute and database reads. The trade-off is staleness/consistency: cached features or predictions can become outdated, so the design must define TTLs, invalidation strategies, and acceptable freshness for the use case.
questions.explanation: This tests the fundamental purpose and trade-offs of caching: performance and cost vs. freshness/consistency. For ML engineers, it’s common to cache embeddings, feature vectors, and even inference outputs for idempotent requests. A strong answer mentions both benefits (latency, throughput, cost) and the key drawback (staleness) plus mechanisms (TTL/invalidation).

questions.question_text: You need to pick a metric to capture user-facing latency for an online ML inference service. Which choice best reflects a fundamental system-design best practice?
options: A. Use average latency only, because it is easiest to compute
B. Use p95/p99 latency (tail latency) in addition to average, because user experience is dominated by slow outliers
C. Use minimum latency, because it shows the fastest possible performance
D. Use CPU utilization as a proxy for latency
questions.explanation: Tail latency (p95/p99) is fundamental in distributed systems because a small fraction of slow requests can dominate perceived performance and often indicates queuing, GC pauses, noisy neighbors, or downstream slowness. Average latency can hide these issues. Minimum latency and CPU utilization are not user-facing latency measures and can be misleading when diagnosing performance.

questions.question_text: In system design, what is the most accurate definition of a "Service Level Objective (SLO)" for an ML inference API?
options: A. A hard guarantee the system must always meet, otherwise it is considered down
B. A measurable target (e.g., p95 latency < X ms, availability > Y%) that the team aims to meet over a time window
C. A list of features the model must support before launch
D. The maximum throughput the system can achieve during load testing
questions.explanation: An SLO is a measurable reliability/latency target over a defined period (e.g., 99.9% availability per month, p95 latency < 200ms). It is not an absolute guarantee (that’s closer to a strict requirement), and it’s distinct from product features and from capacity metrics like peak throughput. For ML inference, common SLOs include tail latency (p95/p99), error rate, and availability, because these directly reflect user experience and operational reliability.

questions.question_text: Which fundamental pattern best reduces cascading failures when a downstream dependency (e.g., feature store or vector DB) becomes slow during online inference?
options: A. Increase request timeouts so calls wait longer and eventually succeed
B. Use a circuit breaker and bulkheads (isolation) to fail fast and limit resource exhaustion
C. Disable load balancing so requests stick to the same replica
D. Use a single shared global lock to serialize requests
questions.explanation: Circuit breakers prevent repeatedly calling an unhealthy dependency, and bulkheads isolate resources so one slow path doesn’t consume all threads/connection pools, which would otherwise cause a cascading failure. Increasing timeouts often worsens queueing and thread exhaustion. Sticky routing and global locks generally reduce resilience and throughput.

questions.question_text: In designing a reliable online inference system, what is the fundamental difference between "availability" and "reliability" (or "correctness"), and how can a system be highly available but still "bad" for users?
questions.correct_answer: Availability is the fraction of time the system responds to requests (e.g., returns a response within some timeout). Reliability/correctness is whether the system returns the right/acceptable result and meets quality constraints. A system can be highly available but bad if it returns fast error fallbacks, stale models, incorrect predictions, or degraded-quality outputs while still responding (e.g., always returning a default class to avoid timeouts). Good design separates uptime SLOs from quality SLOs (accuracy, freshness, calibration, safety) and monitors both.
questions.explanation: This is a fundamental distinction: returning *something* is not the same as returning the *right* thing. ML systems often have additional correctness dimensions (model version, feature freshness, data drift, safety filters). A design that optimizes only availability can silently degrade user experience.

questions.question_text: A model server uses micro-batching to increase GPU utilization. What is the fundamental trade-off micro-batching introduces?
options: A. Higher throughput at the cost of higher latency (especially tail latency) due to waiting to form batches
B. Lower throughput but lower latency because the GPU is used less
C. Higher throughput and lower latency because batching always improves both
D. No trade-off; batching only affects cost, not latency or throughput
questions.explanation: Micro-batching improves throughput and cost efficiency by amortizing overhead and better utilizing the GPU, but it can increase latency because requests may wait in a queue until a batch is formed. This can significantly affect tail latency depending on batch window, traffic rate, and batch size limits—fundamental considerations in inference serving.

questions.question_text: When designing an ML inference API, why is idempotency a fundamental property for certain endpoints (e.g., "log this event", "enqueue this job"), and how can you implement it?
questions.correct_answer: Idempotency means repeating the same request has the same effect as doing it once. It’s fundamental for reliability because retries happen (timeouts, network errors, client retries) and you don’t want duplicate side effects (duplicate jobs, double billing, duplicate logs). You can implement it by requiring an idempotency key (unique request ID) and storing a record of processed keys (with TTL) so duplicates return the original result; or by designing operations as upserts with deterministic identifiers; or using deduplication in the queue/consumer.
questions.explanation: Retries are unavoidable in distributed systems. Idempotency is a core reliability fundamental that prevents “at least once” delivery from turning into duplicate side effects. ML systems often enqueue async feature computation, logging, or batch inference jobs—idempotency keeps these pipelines correct under failure.

questions.question_text: You need to pick a caching strategy for an embedding service where many requests repeat (same text input). Which fundamental caching concept best helps you decide if caching will be effective?
options: A. Cache locality (temporal/spatial locality) and expected hit rate
B. CAP theorem for distributed systems
C. Two-phase commit for transactions
D. Leader election algorithms
questions.explanation: Caching effectiveness depends on locality: repeated requests over time (temporal locality) and repeated access patterns. The fundamental metric is hit rate (and the cost saved per hit). CAP, 2PC, and leader election are important distributed fundamentals, but they’re not the primary first principle for deciding whether caching embeddings will pay off.

questions.question_text: In a basic load-balanced inference setup, what is the key difference between "stateless" and "stateful" services, and why do ML inference endpoints often aim to be stateless?
questions.correct_answer: A stateless service does not store per-client/session state on the server between requests; any replica can handle any request. A stateful service keeps state (e.g., session context, conversation history, cached user features) tied to a specific instance or storage. Inference endpoints often aim to be stateless so they can be easily scaled horizontally, load-balanced without sticky sessions, restarted safely, and made more resilient. If state is needed (e.g., chat history), it’s typically stored in an external system (DB/cache) so replicas remain replaceable.
questions.explanation: Statelessness is a fundamental design property that simplifies scaling and reliability. For ML inference, being stateless helps autoscaling, rolling deployments, and failover. When state is unavoidable, externalizing it is a common fundamental pattern.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes the fundamental trade-off between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling for an ML inference service?
options: A. Vertical scaling improves availability because there are fewer machines
B. Horizontal scaling improves fault tolerance and can improve throughput, but adds coordination and load-balancing complexity
C. Horizontal scaling always reduces latency because more machines means faster single-request execution
D. Vertical scaling is always cheaper than horizontal scaling for the same throughput
questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling (more replicas) generally improves throughput and resilience to single-node failure, but introduces fundamentals like load balancing, replica consistency, warm-up/cold-start behavior, and more complex deployments. Vertical scaling (bigger machine/GPU) can simplify coordination and sometimes reduce per-request latency, but increases blast radius and has hardware limits.

questions.question_text: You have an inference endpoint with a 99th percentile latency SLO of 300 ms. Requests arrive at 100 RPS. Each model inference takes ~50 ms of compute time on average. Which is the most likely reason you can still violate the p99 SLO even if average CPU/GPU utilization is only ~40%?
options: A. The model’s average compute time is too small to ever cause p99 issues
B. Queueing effects and burstiness can create long waits even at moderate utilization
C. Lower utilization always increases latency because hardware is underused
D. p99 latency is determined only by network latency, not compute or queueing
questions.explanation: Even with moderate average utilization, bursty arrivals, variability in request sizes, cache misses, and batching behavior can create queueing delays that dominate tail latency. Tail latency is often driven by queueing and variance, not just average compute time. This is a fundamental system design concept for serving ML models: you must account for arrival patterns, per-request variance, and queue management, not only mean utilization.

questions.question_text: In system design, what is the fundamental relationship between throughput, latency, and concurrency described by Little’s Law, and how do you use it when sizing an inference service?
questions.correct_answer: Little’s Law: L = λ × W, where L is average number of items in the system (concurrency/in-flight requests), λ is arrival rate (throughput, e.g., requests/sec), and W is average time in system (latency). To size an inference service, estimate λ and target W (or SLO), then compute required concurrency L. For example, at 200 RPS and 100 ms average latency, L ≈ 200 × 0.1 = 20 concurrent in-flight requests needed (ignoring headroom), then translate that into workers/replicas considering per-replica concurrency limits and utilization targets.
questions.explanation: Little’s Law is a core sizing fundamental: it links how many requests are simultaneously in progress to the rate they arrive and how long they take. It helps prevent common mistakes like assuming you can meet an RPS target with too few workers because you only looked at per-request latency in isolation, ignoring concurrency and queueing.

questions.question_text: You’re designing a simple online inference service for a model. Which metric best represents the "tail latency" that users most often experience and that system designers commonly track to catch worst-case performance?
options: A. Average (mean) latency
B. Median (p50) latency
C. p95 or p99 latency
D. Maximum observed latency
questions.explanation: Tail latency is typically captured using high percentiles (p95/p99). Mean and median can look healthy while a significant fraction of requests are slow (often due to queueing, GC pauses, cold starts, cache misses, or noisy neighbors). Max is too sensitive to outliers and not stable for SLOs; percentiles are the standard fundamental tool for user-perceived worst-case behavior and SLO definition.

questions.question_text:
You have two design choices for serving a model: (1) synchronous request/response, (2) asynchronous with a job queue and callback/polling. Which fundamental tradeoff is most accurate?

A) Async always reduces latency because queues are faster
B) Sync is always more reliable because it’s simpler
C) Async can improve throughput and smooth bursts via buffering, but it increases end-to-end latency and requires handling job state/idempotency
D) There is no difference; both are equivalent
options: A) Async always reduces latency because queues are faster
B) Sync is always more reliable because it’s simpler
C) Async can improve throughput and smooth bursts via buffering, but it increases end-to-end latency and requires handling job state/idempotency
D) There is no difference; both are equivalent
questions.explanation: A fundamental system design tradeoff is synchronous simplicity/low perceived latency vs asynchronous buffering/decoupling. Queues can absorb bursts and prevent overload (improving stability and throughput), but they introduce waiting time and require additional fundamentals: job identifiers, idempotency, retries, dead-letter queues, and state tracking. Neither approach is universally better; it depends on SLOs and workload shape.

questions.question_text: Free text: You’re asked to design a minimal set of core metrics (fundamentals) to monitor a production inference API. List at least 6 metrics and briefly state what each tells you.
questions.correct_answer: Example core set: (1) Request rate (RPS/QPS): demand and traffic shifts. (2) Error rate (5xx/4xx, model errors): reliability and correctness signals. (3) Latency p50/p95/p99: user experience and tail behavior. (4) Saturation/utilization (CPU, GPU, memory): capacity headroom and bottlenecks. (5) Queue length / time in queue (if batching/async): load-induced delays and backpressure needs. (6) Downstream dependency latency/error (feature store, DB, vector DB): identifies external bottlenecks. Optional but common: (7) Cache hit rate: effectiveness of caching. (8) Model-specific metrics like time per token / tokens/sec (LLMs): performance characteristics. (9) Instance count / autoscaler events: scaling behavior.
questions.explanation: These are foundational observability metrics aligned with the classic 'RED' (Rate, Errors, Duration) plus saturation and dependency health. For AI/ML systems, queueing and accelerator utilization are especially important because batching and GPU contention can create tail-latency spikes without obvious average CPU signals.

questions.question_text: Free text: Explain the fundamental difference between throughput and latency in an ML inference system, and give one practical example of a change that improves throughput but can worsen latency (or tail latency).
questions.correct_answer: Latency is the time for a single request to complete (often measured as p50/p95/p99), while throughput is the rate of completed requests over time (e.g., requests/sec). A common example is increasing batch size or enabling aggressive batching on the GPU: it can raise throughput by amortizing overhead and improving hardware utilization, but it can increase per-request waiting time in the batch queue, worsening p95/p99 latency.
questions.explanation: System design fundamentals require separating per-request experience (latency) from system capacity (throughput). Many optimizations trade one for the other. Batching is especially relevant for AI/ML serving: it increases compute efficiency but introduces queueing delay, which shows up most strongly in tail latency under variable traffic.

questions.question_text:
In system design fundamentals, what is the most important reason to keep an inference service stateless?

A) It makes the model more accurate
B) It simplifies horizontal scaling, failover, and rolling deployments because any instance can handle any request
C) It guarantees zero latency
D) It eliminates the need for monitoring
options: A) It makes the model more accurate
B) It simplifies horizontal scaling, failover, and rolling deployments because any instance can handle any request
C) It guarantees zero latency
D) It eliminates the need for monitoring
questions.explanation: Statelessness is a foundational design principle: if instances don’t hold unique session state, traffic can be routed to any healthy replica, enabling easy scaling, self-healing, and deployments with minimal coordination. It doesn’t change model accuracy, doesn’t guarantee low latency, and monitoring remains essential.

questions.question_text:
You’re setting timeouts and retries for calls from your inference service to a downstream feature service. Which fundamental guideline is most correct?

A) Always retry indefinitely; it increases success rate
B) Use retries with exponential backoff and jitter, and set a timeout budget aligned to your end-to-end latency SLO
C) Disable timeouts; they cause failures
D) Retry immediately many times; faster is better
options: A) Always retry indefinitely; it increases success rate
B) Use retries with exponential backoff and jitter, and set a timeout budget aligned to your end-to-end latency SLO
C) Disable timeouts; they cause failures
D) Retry immediately many times; faster is better
questions.explanation: Timeouts and retries are fundamental reliability tools, but naive retries can amplify outages (retry storms) and worsen tail latency. Exponential backoff with jitter reduces synchronization and load spikes. Timeouts should be chosen as part of an overall latency budget so downstream calls don’t consume the entire SLO. Indefinite or immediate repeated retries are classic anti-patterns.

questions.question_text:
In an ML feature retrieval path (online inference), which is the most fundamental reason to use caching?

A) To improve model accuracy
B) To reduce repeated expensive work and lower latency/load for frequently accessed features
C) To avoid writing unit tests
D) To eliminate the need for a database entirely
options: A) To improve model accuracy
B) To reduce repeated expensive work and lower latency/load for frequently accessed features
C) To avoid writing unit tests
D) To eliminate the need for a database entirely
questions.explanation: Caching is a core system design fundamental used to trade memory for speed: it reduces repeated computation and backend calls, improving latency and increasing throughput. It does not inherently improve model accuracy (though it can improve freshness/consistency if done right), and it doesn’t replace durable storage—caches are typically best-effort and can be evicted.

questions.question_text:
You need to choose a basic scaling strategy for a stateless model-serving API. Which statement is most fundamentally correct?

A) Vertical scaling is always better because it reduces networking
B) Horizontal scaling is typically preferred for stateless services because it improves availability and throughput by adding replicas
C) You should avoid load balancers because they add latency
D) Scaling only matters for training jobs, not inference
options: A) Vertical scaling is always better because it reduces networking
B) Horizontal scaling is typically preferred for stateless services because it improves availability and throughput by adding replicas
C) You should avoid load balancers because they add latency
D) Scaling only matters for training jobs, not inference
questions.explanation: For system design fundamentals, stateless services are natural candidates for horizontal scaling: add replicas behind a load balancer to increase throughput and reduce the impact of single-instance failures. Vertical scaling can help but has limits (cost, hardware ceilings, blast radius). Load balancers add small overhead but are essential for distributing load and health-based routing. Inference absolutely needs scaling considerations.

questions.question_text:
You have a GPU-backed inference endpoint. Under load, latency spikes even though GPU utilization is only ~60%. Which fundamental concept most directly explains this?

A) Amdahl’s Law / bottlenecks outside the GPU (CPU preprocessing, serialization, network, queueing)
B) The model needs more parameters
C) The training dataset is too small
D) The optimizer’s learning rate is too high
options: A) Amdahl’s Law / bottlenecks outside the GPU (CPU preprocessing, serialization, network, queueing)
B) The model needs more parameters
C) The training dataset is too small
D) The optimizer’s learning rate is too high
questions.explanation: A fundamental system design idea is that overall latency is limited by the slowest stage in the pipeline. Even with spare GPU capacity, CPU-side tokenization, feature fetching, request batching/queueing, network overhead, or serialization can dominate. Amdahl’s Law captures that optimizing one component (GPU compute) won’t fix end-to-end latency if other components are the bottleneck. The other options are model training concerns, not serving-system fundamentals.

questions.question_text:
In a basic system design discussion, what does it mean to design for an SLO like “99.9% of requests under 300ms”?

A) You only need to optimize mean latency
B) You must meet the target for all requests (100%)
C) You define an acceptable error budget and engineer to meet it over a time window
D) You can ignore failures as long as latency is low
options: A) You only need to optimize mean latency
B) You must meet the target for all requests (100%)
C) You define an acceptable error budget and engineer to meet it over a time window
D) You can ignore failures as long as latency is low
questions.explanation: An SLO is a probabilistic target over a defined time window (e.g., 30 days). “99.9% under 300ms” implies an error budget (0.1% can exceed 300ms) that you can spend during incidents, deploys, and load spikes. System design fundamentals include defining SLO/SLI, measuring them, and making tradeoffs (capacity, caching, timeouts, fallbacks) to stay within the budget. It’s not about mean latency, not 100% perfection, and failures still matter as part of availability SLOs.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing an online inference service for an ML model. Which metric is the most fundamental to track for user-perceived performance at the API boundary?

A) Training throughput (samples/sec)
B) p95/p99 request latency
C) Number of model parameters
D) Size of the training dataset
options: A) Training throughput (samples/sec)
B) p95/p99 request latency
C) Number of model parameters
D) Size of the training dataset
questions.explanation: In system design fundamentals, user experience is driven by end-to-end request latency, and tail latency (p95/p99) is crucial because it captures worst-case behavior under real workloads (queueing, GC pauses, cold caches, noisy neighbors). Average latency can look fine while a significant fraction of users experience slow responses. Training throughput, parameter count, and dataset size matter for model development, but they don’t directly measure the serving system’s responsiveness at the API boundary.

questions.question_text: You need to design monitoring for an online ML inference system. Which set of signals best represents fundamental observability for system health (not model quality)?
options: A. Accuracy, AUC, and F1 score only
B. Request rate (RPS), error rate, and latency percentiles (e.g., p95/p99)
C. Number of model parameters and embedding dimension
D. Training loss curve and learning rate schedule
questions.explanation: For system health, the fundamentals are the 'golden signals': traffic (RPS), errors, and latency (often plus saturation). Model quality metrics matter, but they answer a different question (is the model good?) rather than (is the system reliably serving?). Both are needed, but system design fundamentals start with service SLIs.

questions.question_text: In system design, what is the fundamental purpose of a load balancer in front of multiple inference replicas, and what is one key health-check behavior you would require for ML inference services?
questions.correct_answer: A load balancer distributes incoming requests across replicas to increase throughput, improve availability, and enable rolling deployments. A key health-check requirement is that it verifies the replica is actually ready to serve predictions (readiness), not just that the process is running (liveness). For example, readiness should fail if the model weights are not loaded, the GPU runtime is unavailable, or critical dependencies (like feature store connectivity if required) are down.
questions.explanation: This is a fundamental reliability pattern: without correct readiness checks, traffic can be sent to instances that are up but not capable of correct/fast inference, causing elevated errors and tail latency during deploys, autoscaling, or recovery.

questions.question_text: An inference service experiences periodic latency spikes. Which fundamental approach most directly reduces tail latency under bursty traffic while keeping average latency reasonable?
options: A. Increase log verbosity to debug faster
B. Add a queue with controlled concurrency (backpressure) and optionally batch requests where appropriate
C. Disable timeouts so requests can always complete
D. Use a single larger instance to avoid load balancing
questions.explanation: Backpressure is a fundamental stability mechanism: it prevents overload collapse by controlling concurrency. Queues can smooth bursts, and batching can improve hardware utilization (especially on GPUs) at the cost of added waiting time—often a net win for tail latency and throughput when tuned carefully.

questions.question_text: You’re choosing between strong consistency and eventual consistency for an online feature store backing real-time inference. Describe the fundamental trade-off and give one practical example where eventual consistency is acceptable and one where it is risky.
questions.correct_answer:
Strong consistency ensures reads reflect the latest successful write (within the consistency model), simplifying correctness but often increasing latency and reducing availability under partitions. Eventual consistency allows replicas to converge over time, improving availability/latency but risking stale reads.

Eventual consistency acceptable example: non-critical personalization features (e.g., recently viewed items) where a slightly stale feature rarely harms users.

Eventual consistency risky example: fraud/credit decisioning or safety-critical ranking where stale features (e.g., latest chargeback flag) can cause incorrect approvals or unsafe outcomes.
questions.explanation: This is a core distributed systems fundamental (CAP-style trade-offs). For ML, the cost of staleness depends on how sensitive predictions are to the freshest signals and the business risk of wrong decisions.

questions.question_text: A candidate claims: "We can guarantee exactly-once processing for an ML event pipeline by just turning on retries." What is the most accurate fundamental correction?
options: A. Retries alone typically provide at-least-once delivery; exactly-once requires deduplication/idempotency and careful state management
B. Retries alone typically provide at-most-once delivery; exactly-once is impossible
C. Retries alone provide exactly-once if you use HTTP instead of Kafka
D. Retries alone provide exactly-once if you increase the timeout
questions.explanation: Retries commonly lead to duplicates (at-least-once). Exactly-once semantics require additional mechanisms: idempotent consumers/producers, transactional writes, deduplication keys, or atomic commit of offsets and outputs. This fundamental distinction matters for feature pipelines, training data generation, and online updates.

questions.question_text: You’re designing an inference API. Which choice best describes the fundamental trade-off between synchronous and asynchronous inference?
options: A. Synchronous inference always costs less than asynchronous inference
B. Asynchronous inference improves user-perceived latency for the same request

C. Synchronous inference returns the prediction in the request-response path; asynchronous inference decouples request submission from result retrieval, improving resilience to long-running jobs and smoothing load
D. Asynchronous inference cannot be monitored with SLIs/SLOs
questions.explanation: Synchronous inference is simplest: the client waits for a response. Asynchronous inference (queue + worker + callback/polling) is a fundamental pattern when inference can be slow, bursty, or requires batching; it can improve system stability and throughput at the cost of complexity and delayed results.

questions.question_text: In a distributed ML system, what is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling, and which one typically helps you handle increased inference QPS more effectively?
questions.correct_answer: Vertical scaling increases resources on a single machine (more CPU/GPU/RAM), while horizontal scaling adds more machines/replicas. For increased inference QPS, horizontal scaling is typically more effective because you can add replicas behind a load balancer to increase total capacity and improve availability. Vertical scaling can help when a single request needs more compute (e.g., very large models), but it has limits and can reduce fault tolerance.
questions.explanation: This is a core systems fundamental: scale-out generally improves throughput and resilience (more replicas, smaller blast radius), while scale-up can reduce coordination overhead but hits hardware ceilings and can create single points of failure.

questions.question_text: You need to choose a storage approach for ML features used during online inference. Which fundamental principle helps prevent training-serving skew?
options: A. Use different feature definitions for training and serving to optimize each path independently
B. Ensure the same feature computation logic and definitions are used for both training and serving (or are derived from a shared source of truth)
C. Compute features only at inference time to ensure they are always fresh
D. Store features only in the training data lake and fetch them from there during inference
questions.explanation: Training-serving skew happens when the data or transformations used at training differ from those used at serving. A fundamental mitigation is to share feature definitions and transformation code (or ensure both are generated from the same spec), and to version features so training and serving use compatible definitions.

questions.question_text: In system design, what does it mean for a component (e.g., an inference endpoint) to be "idempotent," and why is it a fundamental requirement for reliable retry behavior?
questions.correct_answer: Idempotent means that performing the same operation multiple times with the same input has the same effect as performing it once. It’s fundamental for retries because network timeouts and transient failures can cause clients or gateways to repeat requests; if the operation is idempotent, retries won’t create duplicated side effects (e.g., double-writing a record, double-charging, double-enqueueing). For inference, pure prediction calls are usually idempotent; problems arise when the endpoint also logs, writes, or triggers downstream actions—those side effects should be deduplicated (e.g., via request IDs) or made idempotent.
questions.explanation: Retries are a core reliability mechanism. Without idempotency (or explicit deduplication), retries can corrupt state and create hard-to-debug inconsistencies. A common fundamental pattern is to attach a unique request ID and ensure downstream writes are 'upsert' or deduplicated based on that ID.

questions.question_text: You’re designing an online inference service for an ML model. Which metric best captures the user-perceived responsiveness of the system and should typically be tracked as a primary SLO for the serving path?
options: A. Throughput (requests per second)
B. End-to-end latency (e.g., p95/p99 request latency)
C. CPU utilization of the inference pods
D. Model size on disk (MB/GB)
questions.explanation: User experience is most directly impacted by end-to-end latency, typically tracked at tail percentiles (p95/p99) because they reflect worst-case experiences under load. Throughput and CPU utilization are important capacity signals, but they are indirect. Model size can influence latency and cost, but it is not a direct user-perceived SLO.

questions.question_text: Why is idempotency a fundamental concept for an online inference API that may be retried by clients or gateways? Provide a concrete example of an idempotency key strategy.
questions.correct_answer: Idempotency ensures that repeating the same request (due to retries/timeouts) does not cause unintended side effects (e.g., duplicate writes, double billing, duplicated feedback signals). For an inference API, predictions themselves are usually safe to recompute, but side effects like logging feedback labels, incrementing counters, or writing feature updates must not be applied twice. A common strategy is: client sends an Idempotency-Key (UUID) per logical operation; the server stores the key with the outcome (or a hash of the request + response) in a fast store with TTL. On retry with the same key, the server returns the stored result and does not re-apply side effects. The key can be scoped by (user_id, model_version, request_fingerprint) depending on the operation.
questions.explanation: Retries are a fundamental reality in distributed systems. Without idempotency, reliability mechanisms (timeouts/retries) can corrupt state. Idempotency keys turn an unreliable network into safe repeated execution by making side-effecting operations deduplicated at the server boundary.

questions.question_text: In distributed systems, what is the fundamental difference between "at-least-once" and "exactly-once" delivery semantics, and why does it matter for an ML pipeline that writes training examples to a dataset?
questions.correct_answer: At-least-once means a message/event may be delivered more than once (duplicates possible) but is not lost; exactly-once means each event affects the system state exactly one time (no loss and no duplicates), which is much harder to guarantee end-to-end. For ML data pipelines, at-least-once can introduce duplicate training examples (biasing counts, skewing class balance, leaking repeated labels) unless you implement idempotent writes/deduplication (e.g., unique event IDs, upserts, watermarking). Exactly-once may be desired for correctness but often increases complexity and cost; many pipelines choose at-least-once plus dedup as the practical fundamental trade-off.
questions.explanation: Delivery semantics drive data correctness. ML systems are particularly sensitive because small systematic duplication can change learned distributions. The fundamental design move is to assume duplicates can happen and design idempotent consumers or dedup mechanisms unless you truly need (and can afford) exactly-once guarantees.

questions.question_text:
Consider this simplified queueing scenario for an inference service:

- Average arrival rate (λ): 900 requests/second
- Each replica can serve (μ): 300 requests/second at steady state

Ignoring variance, what is the minimum number of replicas needed to avoid unbounded queue growth, and what fundamental principle is being applied?
options: A. 2 replicas; applying CAP theorem.
B. 3 replicas; applying the stability condition that total service capacity must exceed arrival rate (utilization < 1).
C. 4 replicas; applying strong consistency requirements.
D. 1 replica; applying batching to increase throughput automatically.
questions.explanation: Total capacity is N * μ. To avoid unbounded queues in steady state, you need N * μ > λ (utilization < 1). Here, 3 * 300 = 900 is exactly equal; in practice you’d want more than 3 to handle variance and bursts, but the minimum to meet the basic stability condition is 3. This is a fundamental queueing/capacity planning idea often used in system design interviews.

questions.question_text: A/B testing and canary releases are often used for model rollouts. As a fundamental system design concept, what problem do they primarily solve in production ML systems?
questions.correct_answer: They reduce deployment risk by limiting blast radius and enabling measurement before full rollout. Canary releases send a small fraction of traffic to the new model/version to detect regressions (latency, error rate, resource use) and functional issues. A/B testing splits traffic to compare business/ML metrics (e.g., CTR, conversion, calibration) under controlled conditions. Both provide a safe feedback loop and rollback path, which is fundamental because offline metrics often do not perfectly predict online performance.
questions.explanation: In ML, correctness is not binary and performance can drift with real user traffic. Canary/A-B are fundamental rollout mechanisms that combine safety (progressive exposure) with observability (metric comparison) to make changes reversible and evidence-based.

questions.question_text: Fundamentally, what is the difference between vertical scaling and horizontal scaling, and which is typically preferred for an ML inference service that must handle traffic spikes?
options: A. Vertical scaling makes a single node bigger; horizontal scaling adds more nodes. Horizontal scaling is typically preferred for spikes because you can add replicas and improve fault tolerance.
B. Vertical scaling adds more nodes; horizontal scaling makes a single node bigger. Vertical scaling is preferred for spikes because it is always cheaper.
C. Vertical scaling means adding GPUs; horizontal scaling means adding CPUs. CPUs are preferred for spikes.
D. Vertical scaling is for training; horizontal scaling is for inference. Training is preferred for spikes.
questions.explanation: Vertical scaling increases resources on one machine (more CPU/RAM/GPU), while horizontal scaling adds more machines/replicas. For spiky inference traffic, horizontal scaling is a fundamental approach because it increases capacity in increments, reduces single-node failure impact, and works well with load balancers and stateless services. Vertical scaling has limits and can create large blast radius.

questions.question_text: You need to ensure an inference request does not hang indefinitely when calling a downstream embedding service. Which fundamental reliability pattern best addresses this, and why?
options: A. Timeouts (often paired with retries/backoff) to bound waiting time and prevent resource exhaustion from stuck calls.
B. Increasing the request payload size to amortize overhead.
C. Using a single shared global mutex to serialize downstream calls.
D. Disabling health checks so the load balancer doesn’t remove instances.
questions.explanation: Timeouts are a core reliability fundamental: they bound how long resources are held and prevent cascading failures. Inference systems often compose multiple services; without timeouts, hung dependencies can exhaust thread pools/event loops, causing a meltdown. Retries must be used carefully (jitter, backoff, budgets) to avoid amplifying load. The other options harm scalability or reliability.

questions.question_text: Which statement best captures the fundamental CAP trade-off as it applies to an ML feature store used for online inference?
options: A. Under a network partition, you must choose between consistency and availability; many online feature stores prefer availability with eventual consistency for some features, depending on correctness requirements.
B. CAP means you can only have two of: CPU, RAM, and GPU, so feature stores should be CPU-heavy.
C. CAP means caching always improves availability but reduces consistency, so caching must be avoided.
D. CAP applies only to relational databases, not to feature stores.
questions.explanation: CAP is a fundamental distributed systems concept: in the presence of partitions, a system must trade off consistency vs availability. For online features, some teams accept slightly stale values (eventual consistency) to keep serving predictions, while other features (e.g., fraud counters) may require strong consistency. CAP is not about compute resources (B), caching is not inherently inconsistent (C), and CAP applies broadly to distributed systems (D).

questions.question_text: A candidate says: "To reduce p99 latency, we should optimize the average latency." As a system design fundamental, why is that not necessarily true, and what should you examine instead?
questions.correct_answer: Average latency can improve while p99 stays the same or worsens because tail latency is dominated by outliers (queueing under burst, GC pauses, cold starts, noisy neighbors, retries, slow downstream dependencies). To reduce p99 you should examine the latency distribution and tail contributors: queue depth and service time variance, saturation points (CPU/GPU/memory), downstream timeouts, retry storms, load balancing skew, and cold-cache/cold-model effects. Use percentiles (p95/p99), SLOs, and profiling/tracing to identify and remove tail sources (e.g., cap queueing, add admission control, isolate resources, warm pools, optimize slow paths).
questions.explanation: Averages hide variability. In production ML systems, p99 is often driven by queueing and rare slow paths, not the median. Fundamental performance design uses distributions and bottleneck analysis (Little’s Law intuition: higher utilization increases queueing non-linearly). Tail-focused mitigation targets the causes of outliers rather than just speeding up the common case.

questions.question_text: You are designing an online inference endpoint. What is the fundamental difference between throughput and latency, and which metric is most directly impacted by increasing batch size on a GPU model server?
options: A. Throughput is requests/second; latency is time per request. Increasing batch size typically improves throughput but can increase per-request latency (especially tail latency).
B. Throughput is time per request; latency is requests/second. Increasing batch size decreases both.
C. Throughput and latency are the same metric; increasing batch size affects only cost.
D. Throughput is network bandwidth; latency is GPU utilization. Increasing batch size affects only network bandwidth.
questions.explanation: Latency measures how long a request takes end-to-end; throughput measures how many requests the system can handle per unit time. Batching often increases GPU efficiency (more work per kernel launch), improving throughput. But it can add queueing/wait time while forming batches, increasing average and tail latency. Understanding this trade-off is fundamental in ML serving.

questions.question_text: In system design, what does it mean for a service to be "stateless," and why is this fundamental for scaling an ML inference API behind a load balancer?
options: A. The service stores no per-request or per-client session state in its own memory/disk, so any instance can handle any request, enabling horizontal scaling and simpler failover.
B. The service never reads from a database, so it can scale without I/O bottlenecks.
C. The service uses only CPU (no GPU), so instances are interchangeable and easy to add.
D. The service cannot cache anything, so responses are always computed from scratch.
questions.explanation: A stateless service does not rely on local instance state to handle requests (e.g., sessions, in-memory user context). This is fundamental because a load balancer can route any request to any healthy instance, making horizontal scaling and failover straightforward. Stateless does not mean "no database" (B), "no GPU" (C), or "no caching" (D). Caching is allowed as long as correctness doesn’t depend on it (i.e., cache misses don’t break the request).

questions.question_text: Free response: In system design fundamentals, explain the difference between scaling up and scaling out for an inference service, and give one practical reason an AI/ML engineer might prefer each (include at least one constraint like GPU memory, model size, or deployment complexity).
questions.correct_answer: Scaling up means using a larger machine (more powerful GPU, more GPU memory, more CPU/RAM) to handle more work per node; scaling out means adding more nodes/replicas. Prefer scaling up when the model (or batch) is constrained by GPU memory or benefits from faster interconnect within a single host (e.g., fitting a large model on one bigger GPU, or reducing cross-node communication). Prefer scaling out when you need higher availability and horizontal throughput, want simpler failure isolation, and can run the model independently per replica (stateless inference) behind a load balancer. Constraints: scaling up hits limits/cost and can create a bigger blast radius; scaling out increases coordination/operational complexity (load balancing, warmup, autoscaling, cache consistency) but improves resilience and elastic capacity.
questions.explanation: This is a fundamentals question because many design decisions reduce to vertical vs horizontal scaling trade-offs. ML-specific constraints (model size, GPU memory, warmup time, batch/concurrency behavior) strongly influence which approach is feasible and cost-effective, and they affect reliability (blast radius) and operations (rolling deploys, autoscaling).

questions.question_text:
You’re choosing between synchronous and asynchronous patterns for a model scoring API. Which is the most fundamental rule-of-thumb?

A. Always use async because it is more scalable
B. Use synchronous request/response when users need immediate results within a strict latency SLO; use async (job + callback/polling) when work is long-running or variable and you need to decouple user latency from processing time
C. Use async only when the model is on GPU
D. Use sync only for batch inference
options: A. Always use async because it is more scalable

B. Use synchronous request/response when users need immediate results within a strict latency SLO; use async (job + callback/polling) when work is long-running or variable and you need to decouple user latency from processing time
C. Use async only when the model is on GPU
D. Use sync only for batch inference
questions.explanation: A fundamental design choice is aligning interaction pattern with latency expectations and variability. Synchronous APIs are appropriate when you can reliably meet latency SLOs and the caller needs the result immediately (e.g., ranking). Asynchronous workflows handle long or spiky jobs (large documents, long generations, heavy preprocessing) by decoupling submission from completion, improving resilience and smoothing load.

questions.question_text:
When designing a caching layer for an inference service, which statement reflects the most fundamental constraint that determines cache effectiveness?

A. Cache hit rate depends primarily on the programming language used
B. Cache effectiveness depends on request repeatability and key design (i.e., whether identical inputs occur and can be keyed deterministically)
C. Caching always improves p99 latency regardless of workload
D. Caching is only useful for offline batch inference
options: A. Cache hit rate depends primarily on the programming language used
B. Cache effectiveness depends on request repeatability and key design (i.e., whether identical inputs occur and can be keyed deterministically)
C. Caching always improves p99 latency regardless of workload
D. Caching is only useful for offline batch inference
questions.explanation: Caching fundamentals: you only get value if requests repeat and you can map them to stable keys. For ML inference, that often means canonicalizing inputs (normalizing text, consistent tokenization/versioning, including model version in the key) and understanding distribution (long tail vs heavy hitters). Caching can hurt tail latency if it adds network hops or contention, and it’s useful in both online and offline contexts when repeatability exists.

questions.question_text: Free response: Explain (at a fundamentals level) how you would do a back-of-the-envelope capacity estimate for an LLM inference endpoint. Include at least: expected QPS, average output tokens, token/sec per GPU, concurrency/queueing assumptions, and how you translate that into number of GPUs and headroom.
questions.correct_answer: A solid fundamentals answer computes demand in tokens/sec and matches it to supply. Steps: (1) Estimate workload: peak QPS, average input tokens and output tokens, plus distribution (p95 output length). (2) Convert to token generation demand: demand_tokens_per_sec ≈ QPS * avg_output_tokens (and optionally include prefill cost from input tokens). (3) Estimate per-GPU throughput for the chosen model/hardware (e.g., 150 tokens/sec/GPU at target batch/concurrency). (4) GPUs_needed ≈ demand_tokens_per_sec / tokens_per_sec_per_gpu, then round up. (5) Add headroom for bursts, tail latency, failures, and rolling deploys (e.g., 30–50% or N+1). (6) Validate latency: if batching/queueing is used, ensure max queue delay + compute time fits the p95/p99 budget; adjust max batch size, max in-flight requests, and autoscaling policy accordingly.
questions.explanation: Capacity planning fundamentals rely on unit analysis and separating demand from supply. For LLMs, the natural unit is tokens/sec (often split into prefill and decode). You also must state assumptions: peak vs average, batch size, concurrency, and acceptable queueing delay. Headroom is fundamental because real systems have variance (burstiness), tail latencies, and partial failures; designing at 100% utilization is brittle.

questions.question_text:
You are asked to design idempotent inference requests because clients may retry on timeouts. Which design is most fundamentally correct?

A. Use POST without a request id; retries will be treated as new requests
B. Include a client-generated idempotency key (request_id) and ensure the server can safely deduplicate or reprocess without side effects
C. Use GET for all inference requests because GET is always idempotent
D. Disable retries at the client to avoid duplicates
options: A. Use POST without a request id; retries will be treated as new requests
B. Include a client-generated idempotency key (request_id) and ensure the server can safely deduplicate or reprocess without side effects
C. Use GET for all inference requests because GET is always idempotent
D. Disable retries at the client to avoid duplicates
questions.explanation: Idempotency is a core API design fundamental in distributed systems. Retries are normal, so requests should be safe to repeat. A client-provided idempotency key lets the server recognize duplicates and either return the same result or ensure repeated processing doesn’t create side effects (e.g., double-charging, double-logging to an exactly-once pipeline). GET being idempotent doesn’t automatically fit inference (payload sizes, privacy, caching semantics), and disabling retries harms reliability.

questions.question_text:
In a distributed online inference system, why are timeouts considered a fundamental design tool?

A. They increase accuracy by limiting model compute time
B. They prevent resource exhaustion and cascading failures by bounding how long work can occupy threads/GPUs/connections
C. They remove the need for retries
D. They guarantee p99 latency will always be below the timeout value
options: A. They increase accuracy by limiting model compute time
B. They prevent resource exhaustion and cascading failures by bounding how long work can occupy threads/GPUs/connections
C. They remove the need for retries
D. They guarantee p99 latency will always be below the timeout value
questions.explanation: Timeouts are fundamental because distributed systems fail in partial, slow, and unpredictable ways. Without timeouts, requests can pile up, saturate thread pools, connection pools, or GPU queues, and trigger cascading failures across services. Timeouts help enforce latency budgets and enable safe retries/fallbacks. They don’t guarantee tail latency (you can still time out), and they don’t eliminate the need for retries; they make retries feasible and bounded.

questions.question_text:
A service has a hard dependency on a feature store. In terms of fundamentals, what is the most appropriate default behavior when the feature store is down for online inference?

A. Fail open: return predictions using default/empty features without signaling
B. Fail closed: return an error (or a degraded response) and surface it via metrics/alerts, unless business requirements explicitly allow a degraded path
C. Block indefinitely until the feature store recovers to preserve correctness
D. Retry forever with exponential backoff in the request path
options: A. Fail open: return predictions using default/empty features without signaling
B. Fail closed: return an error (or a degraded response) and surface it via metrics/alerts, unless business requirements explicitly allow a degraded path
C. Block indefinitely until the feature store recovers to preserve correctness
D. Retry forever with exponential backoff in the request path
questions.explanation: Fundamentals: define correctness and failure modes explicitly. If a dependency is required for correct predictions, silently failing open can create undetected bad outputs. Blocking indefinitely or retrying forever violates latency SLOs and can cascade failures. A principled default is to fail closed (or return a clearly marked degraded response) with bounded retries/timeouts, while monitoring and alerting. If the business explicitly accepts degraded predictions, you can design a controlled fallback (cached features, last-known-good, simpler model) with clear observability.

questions.question_text:
You need to estimate network bandwidth for an embedding service. Each request sends 2KB of JSON and returns a 3,072-dim float32 vector. Peak is 500 QPS. Ignoring overhead and compression, what is the approximate outbound bandwidth (server -> clients) at peak?

A. ~6 MB/s
B. ~60 MB/s
C. ~6 GB/s
D. ~600 KB/s
options: A. ~6 MB/s
B. ~60 MB/s
C. ~6 GB/s
D. ~600 KB/s
questions.explanation: A float32 is 4 bytes. A 3,072-dim vector is 3,072 * 4 = 12,288 bytes ≈ 12 KB. At 500 QPS, outbound payload ≈ 12 KB * 500 = 6,000 KB/s ≈ 6 MB/s. But note the question asks outbound bandwidth and the closest option set includes ~60 MB/s; why? Many candidates miss that 12,288 bytes is ~12.3 KB (not 12 KB) and that practical payloads often include JSON/base64 overhead. However, since the prompt says ignore overhead, the correct fundamental calculation is ~6 MB/s; the closest provided option is A. If forced to choose among the given answers with the stated assumptions, A is correct. (If vectors are returned as JSON arrays of numbers, overhead can easily push this toward tens of MB/s.)

questions.question_text:
In an ML inference API, what is the most fundamental reason to separate "throughput" from "latency" when designing the system?

A. Throughput and latency are always inversely proportional, so you only need one metric
B. Throughput measures total work per unit time, while latency measures time per request; design decisions like batching can improve throughput while worsening tail latency
C. Latency only matters for offline systems; throughput only matters for online systems
