questions.correct_answer: Strategy: (1) Build golden sets representing key scenarios: common intents, edge cases, safety/compliance cases, multilingual, and tenant-specific templates; include expected constraints (tone, required fields, forbidden content). Version the dataset and keep it stable for regression. (2) Use a rubric with multiple dimensions (correctness, completeness, policy compliance, style, hallucination risk). Evaluate with a combination of deterministic checks (regex/structured constraints), human-labeled subsets, and LLM-judge scoring with calibration. Mitigate LLM-judge pitfalls via: fixed judge model/version, prompt/version control, inter-judge agreement checks, spot human audits, and adversarial examples to detect reward hacking. (3) Define regression tests: compare candidate model/prompt against baseline on the golden set; require non-degradation on safety/compliance (hard gates) and bounded degradation on quality metrics; use confidence intervals or repeated runs to handle stochasticity. (4) CI/CD integration: on each change (prompt, retrieval, model), run offline eval pipeline; publish an eval report artifact; block deployment if hard gates fail; allow canary release if soft metrics pass; then monitor online metrics with guardrails and automatic rollback triggers.
questions.explanation: LLM quality is multi-dimensional and stochastic, so relying only on A/B tests is slow and risky. Golden sets create repeatable offline signals; rubrics make evaluation measurable; and regression thresholds provide release gates. LLM-judges are useful but can drift and be gamed, so they need versioning, calibration, and human oversight. Integrating this into CI/CD turns evaluation into an engineering control, not an ad-hoc process.

questions.question_text: Your team wants reproducibility across model training and deployment. Which design is the MOST complete end-to-end governance story for an ML system?
options: A. Save the final model weights in a shared folder and document the training code in a wiki.
B. Track only the Git commit hash of training code; datasets can be regenerated later from the data lake.

C. Use a model registry with immutable model versions, store training config + code commit, dataset version identifiers (snapshots/manifests), feature definitions, evaluation reports, and artifact checksums; capture lineage/provenance so you can reproduce training and audit what served in production.
D. Rely on A/B tests only; if metrics improve, reproducibility is not necessary.
questions.explanation: Reproducibility requires more than code: you need the exact data snapshot/manifests, feature definitions, configs, environment, and evaluation results tied to an immutable model artifact. A model registry plus dataset/versioned manifests and lineage enables audits, rollbacks, and reliable comparisons. A and B are incomplete because data and features drift; D ignores governance needs like debugging, compliance, and regression analysis.

questions.question_text:
You maintain an embedding-based retrieval system (vector DB) for product search. You are rolling out a new embedding model version v2.

Design an embedding lifecycle plan that covers:
- Versioning and compatibility (v1 vs v2)
- Backfilling/re-embedding strategy at scale
- Consistency between source-of-truth catalog and vector index
- Safe rollout and rollback

Be explicit about how queries are handled during the migration.
questions.correct_answer: Plan: (1) Version embeddings explicitly (embedding_model_version, schema/dim, normalization) and store embeddings in a versioned index (separate collections or namespaces: index_v1, index_v2). (2) Dual-write/dual-index: when catalog items change, produce embeddings for both v1 and v2 during migration (or at least ensure v2 is produced) and write to the corresponding index with idempotent upserts. (3) Backfill: run a distributed re-embedding job reading from catalog SOT (or a snapshot) with checkpoints, rate limits, and retries; track coverage metrics (% items embedded, freshness lag). (4) Query handling: either (a) shadow v2 retrieval in parallel and compare metrics while serving v1, then gradually shift traffic to v2; or (b) serve a blended retrieval (retrieve from both indexes and merge) only if needed, noting score calibration issues. (5) Consistency: maintain a change-data-capture pipeline from catalog to embedding jobs; use tombstones/deletes to remove items; periodic reconciliation jobs to detect missing/extra vectors. (6) Rollout/rollback: feature flag routing (per-tenant/percentage), keep v1 index intact until v2 is stable; rollback by flipping routing back to v1. Keep provenance so you can reproduce which index/model served each request.
questions.explanation: Embedding systems fail in practice due to lifecycle issues: partial backfills, mismatched dimensions, stale vectors, and index/catalog drift. Versioned indexes and dual-write prevent mixing incompatible embeddings. A CDC-driven pipeline plus reconciliation keeps the vector store consistent with the source-of-truth. Safe rollout requires shadowing and gradual traffic shifting with the ability to instantly rollback.

questions.question_text: You’re asked to design a recommendation system at an ML-engineer level. Which decomposition and data flow is MOST appropriate for production?
options: A. A single monolithic model that takes all items in the catalog and outputs a full ranking on every request.

B. Two-stage system: candidate generation (fast retrieval using embeddings/heuristics) → ranking (heavier model on ~100–1000 candidates) → re-ranking with business rules; log impressions with model/feature versions and join labels later.
C. Offline training only; at serving time always show the same curated list to avoid latency issues.
D. Only use collaborative filtering; avoid content features because they can introduce leakage.
questions.explanation: Most real recommenders are multi-stage: retrieval narrows the search space cheaply, then ranking applies more expensive models, and re-ranking enforces constraints (diversity, freshness, policy). This structure supports latency budgets, scalability, and iterative improvements. Logging with versions and label joins is essential for training and evaluation alignment. A is computationally infeasible at scale; C isn’t personalized; D is too restrictive and worsens cold start.

questions.question_text:
In a marketplace recommender, new items are added constantly (item cold start). You have:
- Content metadata (title, description, category)
- Some items have images
- Behavioral signals arrive later (impressions/clicks)

Design a practical approach to rank new items reasonably on day 0 and improve over time. Mention candidate generation, ranking features, and how you prevent the system from never giving new items exposure.
questions.correct_answer: Day 0: generate candidates using content-based retrieval (text/image embeddings) and category/constraint filters; use a ranking model that can operate on content + context features (category, price, seller quality priors, freshness) without relying on behavioral aggregates. As interactions arrive, progressively add behavioral features (impression-adjusted CTR priors, dwell time, conversion) with smoothing (Bayesian priors) to avoid volatility. Prevent starvation via an exploration mechanism: reserve a quota for fresh/new items per feed or use a bandit that includes an item-age feature and exploration bonus; also enforce diversity/freshness constraints in re-ranking. Log exposures and propensities so offline training/eval can correct for exposure bias.
questions.explanation: Item cold start is addressed by content-based representations and priors, then transitioning to behavior-informed ranking as data accumulates. Without explicit exploration or freshness constraints, new items can be trapped in a feedback loop where they never get impressions, so they never get clicks, so they never rank. System design includes both retrieval (candidate generation) and ranking, plus policy/constraints to guarantee exposure.

questions.question_text: You’re launching a new personalized feed. For new users with no history, which system-level approach best addresses cold start while controlling feedback loops?
options: A. Show a purely random feed for the first week to avoid bias, then switch to personalization.
B. Use a popularity-only model for new users and never mix in exploration because it hurts short-term CTR.

C. Use a hybrid strategy: start with popularity + context (locale, device, time), incorporate lightweight onboarding signals, and reserve a small exploration budget (e.g., epsilon-greedy or bandit) to collect preference data; log propensities for unbiased learning.
D. Block the user until they answer a long questionnaire so you can fully personalize from day one.
questions.explanation: Cold start is solved by combining priors (popularity), contextual features, and fast signal collection. A small, controlled exploration budget reduces the risk of self-reinforcing feedback loops and helps learn preferences quickly. Logging propensities enables counterfactual evaluation and debiasing. Pure randomness (A) is often too low quality; never exploring (B) slows learning and can entrench popularity bias; heavy onboarding (D) hurts activation.

questions.question_text:
You can only log 5% of inference traffic for an LLM-based support agent due to cost. You still want good coverage for debugging and offline evaluation.

Describe a sampling strategy that balances:
- Rare but important failures (e.g., escalations, policy violations)
- Representative coverage across tenants and locales
- Minimizing privacy exposure

Include what signals you’d use to upsample, and how you’d avoid biasing offline metrics.
questions.correct_answer: Use stratified + priority sampling: (1) Always-log or heavily upsample high-value/rare events: escalations to human, user reports, safety/policy triggers, tool failures, high-latency/timeouts, low-confidence or high-uncertainty outputs, and extreme user dissatisfaction signals. (2) For baseline representativeness, allocate a fixed per-tenant/per-locale sampling budget (e.g., 2% uniform per stratum) to prevent big tenants from dominating. (3) Apply privacy minimization: redact PII fields, store references/hashed IDs, and separate sensitive payloads behind stricter access; optionally store only derived features for most samples and keep full transcripts only for priority events. (4) To avoid biased offline metrics, record the sampling probability (propensity) with each logged event and use inverse propensity weighting when computing offline estimates; maintain a separate uniformly-sampled “metrics slice” used for KPI tracking and regression tests.
questions.explanation: Cost-limited logging is common in ML products. The design goal is two datasets: a representative slice for unbiased measurement and a targeted slice for debugging rare failures. Stratification prevents skew across tenants/locales, while priority sampling ensures you capture long-tail issues. Propensity logging is the key to correcting sampling bias in offline metrics. Privacy-aware logging requires collecting the minimum necessary data, redaction, and strong access controls.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing logging for an online recommendation system to support:
- Offline training
- Debugging bad recommendations
- Online/offline metric alignment
- Privacy compliance (PII minimization)

Which logging schema is the BEST starting point?
options: A. Log only the final ranked list of item IDs and the user ID so you can reproduce what the user saw.
B. Log the full request/response payloads including raw user text, full user profile, and all feature values; store indefinitely for maximum reproducibility.

C. Log an impression event with: anonymized user key, timestamp, request context, candidate set IDs, ranking scores, top-K shown, model/feature versions, and later join with click/conversion labels via a stable event_id; apply field-level redaction and retention limits.
D. Log only aggregate counters (CTR, conversion) per model version to avoid privacy issues and reduce storage.
questions.explanation: A strong ML logging design captures enough to (1) train and evaluate offline, (2) debug and reproduce decisions, and (3) attach ground truth later. That typically means impression-level logs with stable identifiers (event_id), model/feature versions for reproducibility, and the candidate set + scores to analyze ranking. Privacy-aware logging requires minimization (avoid raw PII), redaction/tokenization, access controls, and retention limits. A and D are insufficient for training/debugging; B violates privacy principles and is operationally risky.

questions.question_text:
You run a RAG assistant. Each user question produces:
- 1 prompt + completion pair (average 2.5 KB of text after compression)
- 10 retrieved document IDs (8 bytes each) + scores (4 bytes each)
- 1 embedding vector stored for analysis: 1536 dims FP16
Traffic:
- 150 QPS sustained, 2x peak during business hours
Retention:
- Keep raw logs 14 days, keep embeddings 90 days

Estimate the daily storage growth for (a) raw logs (text + retrieval metadata) and (b) embeddings. Show your math and call out one common mistake in such estimates.
questions.correct_answer:
(a) Raw logs per request ≈ text 2.5 KB + retrieval metadata. Retrieval metadata: 10*(8+4)=120 bytes; add overhead/JSON framing say ~200 bytes → ~2.7 KB/request (order-of-magnitude). Requests/day at sustained 150 QPS: 150*86400 ≈ 12.96M. Daily raw log growth ≈ 12.96M * 2.7 KB ≈ 35,0xx,000 KB ≈ ~35 GB/day (unreplicated). Peak hours increase instantaneous write rate but daily volume is based on average; if average is higher due to peak, adjust accordingly.
(b) Embedding size: 1536 dims * 2 bytes (FP16) = 3072 bytes ≈ 3 KB per request. Daily embedding growth ≈ 12.96M * 3 KB ≈ 38.9 GB/day.
Common mistake: confusing QPS peak with average when computing daily volume, or forgetting that FP16 is 2 bytes per dimension (and also ignoring storage overhead like indexing, replication, and compression).
questions.explanation: ML system design often requires sizing logs and embedding stores. The key steps are: requests/day = QPS * 86400, bytes/request from payload structure, then multiply. Retention affects total stored at rest (daily growth * retention days), while peaks affect write throughput and partitioning but not daily volume unless they change the average. Also remember overheads: schema/JSON, indexes, replication factor, and compaction can dominate raw payload size.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing a real-time text-embedding service for search. Assumptions:
- Peak traffic: 800 QPS
- P95 end-to-end latency SLO: 120 ms
- Model: outputs 1536-dim FP16 embeddings
- Each request embeds one query (no batching initially)
- GPU inference time per query (compute only): 6 ms on a single GPU
- Network + app overhead (non-GPU): 25 ms at P95

(a) Roughly how many GPUs do you need for peak (ignore redundancy)?
(b) What are 2 practical techniques you would add to reduce GPU count while still meeting the latency budget?
questions.correct_answer:
(a) With 6 ms GPU compute per request and no batching, one GPU can do ~1 / 0.006 = ~166 QPS (assuming perfect utilization). For 800 QPS peak, GPUs needed ≈ 800 / 166 ≈ 4.8 → 5 GPUs (then add headroom in real life).
(b) Techniques: (1) Micro-batching with a tight max-wait (e.g., 2–5 ms) to increase throughput without violating P95; (2) Use multiple model instances/streams per GPU + dynamic batching in Triton/TensorRT to improve utilization; (3) Quantization (FP16→INT8) if acceptable; (4) Cache frequent query embeddings; (5) Use a smaller/faster embedding model for tail latency or two-tier models.
questions.explanation: This is a back-of-the-envelope sizing exercise tailored to ML inference. Throughput sizing comes from service time: QPS per GPU ≈ 1 / (GPU time per request) when unbatched. Latency budgeting forces you to consider non-GPU overhead and whether batching adds queueing delay. To reduce GPU count, you typically increase effective throughput per GPU (dynamic batching, better concurrency, quantization) or reduce demand (caching), while keeping P95 within the latency budget by bounding batch wait time and controlling queue depth.

questions.question_text:
Design security/privacy controls for an ML/LLM system that handles PII and includes:
- training data lake
- feature store
- LLM prompts and conversation logs
- vector DB for retrieval

Cover:
- access control model (RBAC/ABAC), least privilege
- secrets management for tools/connectors
- audit trails and access reviews
- PII handling (tokenization/redaction/encryption)
- how retention/deletion policies are enforced technically
questions.correct_answer:
A practical security/privacy design focuses on least privilege, auditable access, and enforceable policies:

1) Access control
- Use RBAC/ABAC: roles (ML engineer, analyst, labeler) + attributes (dataset sensitivity, purpose, region).
- Separate prod vs dev; restrict raw PII to a small approved group.
- Feature-level permissions in the feature store; namespace ownership.

2) Secrets management
- Store API keys/tool credentials in a secrets manager (rotated, scoped).
- Short-lived tokens via workload identity (no long-lived secrets in code).

3) Audit trails + access reviews
- Log data access (who/what/when/purpose) for datasets, feature views, prompt logs, vector collections.
- Periodic access recertification; alert on anomalous access.

4) PII handling
- Classify fields; apply minimization.
- Encrypt at rest and in transit; optionally tokenize sensitive identifiers.
- Redact PII in LLM logs/traces; use structured logging with allowlists.
- For vector DB: store minimal metadata; avoid embedding raw PII where possible; support per-document/user deletion.

5) Retention/deletion enforcement
- Implement TTL/partition expiration where possible.
- Central deletion service orchestrates cascaded deletes across logs/features/vectors/traces; tombstones prevent reintroduction.
- Backups: short retention or key-based encryption to enable cryptographic erasure.

This yields enforceable compliance controls without requiring deep security specialization.
questions.explanation: ML/LLM systems expand the attack surface: data lakes, feature stores, prompt logs, and vector DBs all can leak PII. A credible interview answer ties together access control, secrets, auditability, and policy enforcement (retention/deletion) with concrete mechanisms like ABAC, workload identity, redaction, and tombstones.

questions.question_text:
Multiple choice: Which monitoring setup best distinguishes *data drift* from *performance regression* and supports root-cause analysis in production ML?

Assume you can log features, predictions, and (delayed) labels.
options: A. Track only overall accuracy daily; if it drops, retrain immediately
B. Track feature distribution drift metrics, prediction distribution, and label-based performance metrics by segment; link alerts to feature lineage/owners and recent pipeline/model/prompt changes
C. Track GPU utilization and request latency; if they look fine, the model is healthy
D. Track only prompt tokens and cost; if costs are stable, quality is stable
questions.explanation: Data drift (input distribution changes) and performance regression (metric degradation) are related but distinct. A useful monitoring design includes: feature drift, prediction drift, and label-based metrics when available, all sliced by segments to localize issues. Connecting alerts to lineage/ownership and recent changes enables root-cause workflows instead of blind retraining.

questions.question_text:
Scenario: After a feature pipeline refactor, online AUC drops sharply, but offline validation metrics look unchanged. You suspect training/serving skew.

Describe a step-by-step debugging workflow to find the root cause. Include:
- what logs/metrics you’d inspect
- how you’d test offline-vs-online feature parity
- common skew causes (time windows, joins, missing values, leakage)
- how you’d design guardrails to prevent recurrence (tests, contracts, monitoring)

Be explicit about what evidence would confirm each hypothesis.
questions.correct_answer:
A systematic skew-debug workflow focuses on isolating where semantics diverged:

1) Confirm the symptom and scope
- Compare online KPI drop by segment (region, device, traffic source) to localize.
- Check inference latency/timeouts causing fallback behavior.

2) Verify model artifact parity
- Ensure the same model version and preprocessing config are deployed as used in offline eval.
- Confirm feature list/order and default handling are identical.

3) Inspect feature health online
- Per-feature monitoring: null rate, default rate, freshness/lag, min/max/quantiles.
- Look for sudden distribution shifts starting at deploy time.

4) Offline-vs-online parity test
- Sample real online requests (entity_id + timestamp + request context).
- Recompute features offline using point-in-time logic for the same timestamps.
- Diff feature vectors: exact match for deterministic features; tolerance bounds for approximate/streaming.
- Identify top mismatching features and correlate with prediction deltas.

5) Hypothesis-driven checks
- Time window bug: window anchored to processing time instead of event time; confirm by checking feature values around boundary times.
- Join key mismatch: entity_id normalization changes; confirm via join hit-rate.
- Missing values: online store TTL/refresh issues; confirm via freshness and cache hit metrics.
- Leakage in offline: offline pipeline accidentally used future events; confirm by enforcing strict point-in-time joins and rerunning offline eval.

6) Fix and prevent recurrence
- Single-source feature definitions (registry/DSL) used for both paths.
- Contract tests: schema, key normalization, window semantics.
- Automated parity tests in CI using recorded requests.
- Runtime monitors: feature freshness, null/default spikes, distribution drift alerts.

Evidence-based confirmation comes from parity diffs and aligning the mismatch onset with the refactor deployment.
questions.explanation: This scenario is common: offline metrics can look fine even when online degrades due to feature freshness, join issues, or time semantics. The fastest path is to diff online-request feature vectors against offline recomputation at the same timestamps, then add guardrails (single-source definitions, parity tests, and feature health monitoring).

questions.question_text:
You’re building a human-in-the-loop labeling system for a computer vision model. Labels arrive with delays (hours to days). You want to support active learning and maintain label quality.

Design the labeling ops system:
- queues and task assignment
- annotation tooling and schema/versioning
- QA sampling and adjudication
- throughput/cost controls
- how delayed labels flow back into training and evaluation

Include how you would measure labeler quality and how you’d handle ambiguous cases.
questions.correct_answer:
A practical labeling system includes task orchestration, schema control, and quality loops:

1) Task queue + assignment
- Create labeling tasks from data ingestion or active learning selection.
- Use a queue with priority (uncertain samples, new classes, regressions).
- Assignment policies: skill-based routing, load balancing, rate limits.

2) Tooling + schema/versioning
- Annotation UI with clear guidelines.
- Versioned label schema (classes, bounding box format, attributes). Store schema_version with each label.
- Support “unknown/ambiguous” and free-text notes.

3) QA and adjudication
- Gold set: known-answer tasks to calibrate labelers.
- Ongoing sampling: % of tasks double-labeled; compute inter-annotator agreement.
- Adjudication workflow for conflicts: senior labelers or consensus.

4) Throughput/cost controls
- Batch tasks, cap per-labeler throughput, and monitor time-per-task.
- Active learning to reduce labeling volume by focusing on high-uncertainty/high-impact samples.

5) Delayed labels into ML pipelines
- Store labels in a label store with timestamps and dataset snapshots.
- Training pipeline consumes a consistent snapshot (point-in-time) and records label lag.
- Evaluation: separate “fresh” vs “mature” label sets; avoid leaking future labels.

6) Measuring label quality
- Per-labeler accuracy on gold tasks; disagreement rates; drift over time.
- Track confusion matrices by class and by labeler.

7) Handling ambiguous cases
- Allow ‘cannot determine’ label; route to expert review.
- Update guidelines and schema; backfill if schema changes.

This design keeps labeling reliable and integrates delayed supervision cleanly into training/eval.
questions.explanation: Labeling systems are operations-heavy: queues, tooling, QA, and cost control matter as much as model code. Delayed labels require snapshotting and careful evaluation to avoid leakage. Active learning is only effective if integrated with task prioritization and quality measurement.

questions.question_text:
You’re given an ambiguous prompt in an interview: “Design a real-time personalization system.”

As an AI/ML engineer (not a system design specialist), walk through how you would structure the discussion:
- clarifying questions
- success metrics and constraints
- proposed API(s)
- data flow (online inference, offline training, feature computation)
- bottlenecks and tradeoffs

Provide the outline you’d present on a whiteboard, including what you’d explicitly *not* design in depth.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong interview structure prioritizes framing and clear interfaces:

1) Clarify scope
- What is personalized (rank items? recommend content? pricing?)
- Latency/SLA, QPS, top-K size, cold start expectations.
- Available signals (events, user profile), privacy constraints, regions.
- Evaluation metric (CTR, conversion, revenue) and guardrails (diversity, fairness).

2) Define success metrics/constraints
- Online: p95 latency, error rate, cost per request, business KPI.
- Offline: NDCG/AUC, calibration, freshness.

3) High-level APIs
- GET /recommendations?user_id=...&context=...
- POST /events (clicks, views) with event_time.
- Optional: GET /features or internal feature retrieval.

4) Data flow
- Event ingestion -> streaming/batch feature pipelines -> feature store.
- Offline training pipeline -> model registry -> deployment.
- Online inference service: request -> feature fetch -> model predict -> ranking -> response.

5) Bottlenecks/tradeoffs
- Feature fetch latency vs caching; freshness vs cost.
- Online vs precomputed candidates.
- Consistency: point-in-time correctness for training; parity tests.

6) What not to deep dive
- Detailed cluster sizing, exact DB internals, low-level networking.
- Unless asked, keep to interfaces, correctness, and ML-specific failure modes.

This outline demonstrates structured communication under ambiguity and focuses on ML-relevant system design decisions.
questions.explanation: Interviewers often evaluate whether candidates can frame an ambiguous problem, define APIs and data flows, and reason about tradeoffs. For AI/ML engineers, the key is connecting online inference to offline training and feature computation, plus identifying ML-specific bottlenecks (feature latency, skew, freshness, evaluation).

questions.question_text: Multiple choice: You run an ML inference API in two regions. You need basic disaster recovery with minimal complexity. Which setup is typically the best starting point for an AI/ML engineer to propose (assuming stateless inference containers and a shared model registry)?
options: A. Active-active with global load balancing and multi-master writes to all stateful stores
B. Active-passive with health-checked failover at the load balancer/DNS level and replicated read-only artifacts (models/features) to the passive region
C. Single region only; rely on autoscaling for outages
D. Active-active without any data replication; each region uses its own independent feature store
questions.explanation: For many ML inference services, the simplest DR is active-passive: keep a warm standby region, replicate required artifacts (models, configs, possibly feature data), and fail over via health checks. Active-active can be more complex due to consistency requirements for stateful dependencies (feature stores, vector DBs, rate limits). Independent regional data stores risk inconsistent predictions and hard-to-debug behavior.

questions.question_text:
A user invokes right-to-be-forgotten (RTBF). Your system includes:
- raw event logs in object storage
- a feature store (offline + online)
- an embeddings/vector database used for RAG
- LLM interaction logs and agent traces

Design a deletion workflow that is auditable and minimizes downtime. Explain:
- what “deletion” means for vectors/embeddings and indexes
- how you handle backups and replicas
- how you prevent deleted data from reappearing via reindexing/backfills
- how you verify completion
questions.correct_answer:
A practical RTBF workflow is a coordinated, auditable pipeline with tombstones and reprocessing controls:

1) Orchestration + audit
- A deletion request service creates a deletion job (user_id, scope, deadline, requester, legal basis).
- Append-only audit log of actions taken and systems touched.

2) Raw logs (object storage)
- If logs are immutable, use: (a) per-user partitioning to enable physical deletion, or (b) cryptographic erasure (delete per-user encryption keys) + redaction layers.
- For shared files, run a compaction/redaction job to rewrite affected partitions.

3) Feature store
- Online: delete entity keys for the user; ensure caches are invalidated.
- Offline: delete or rewrite partitions; if using time-travel tables, create redacted snapshots and block access to prior snapshots if required.
- Add a “tombstone” table keyed by user_id to prevent future backfills from re-materializing deleted entities.

4) Vector DB semantics
- Delete vectors by document/user metadata filter (soft delete flag or hard delete).
- If the index doesn’t immediately reclaim, schedule reindex/compaction.
- Ensure replicas receive deletions; for eventual consistency, track completion per shard.

5) Backups/replication
- Define backup retention that meets compliance; support deletion propagation to backups where required or rely on short retention + key-based encryption.
- Multi-region replicas: deletion events replicated like writes; verify per-region completion.

6) Prevent reappearance
- All ingestion/backfill pipelines consult tombstones to drop events for deleted users.
- Reindexing jobs join against tombstones so deleted items are excluded.

7) Verification
- Automated checks: query each system for user_id presence (features, vectors, logs); verify index stats.
- Produce a completion report with timestamps and evidence; alert on failures.

This design balances compliance, operational feasibility, and auditability.
questions.explanation: RTBF is a cross-system problem. The tricky parts are (1) immutable logs and backups, (2) vector index deletion semantics (soft delete vs compaction), and (3) preventing reintroduction via backfills/reindexing. Tombstones plus pipeline enforcement and auditable orchestration are common real-world solutions.

questions.question_text:
Design the caching and state strategy for a conversational assistant that supports:
- multi-turn conversations
- tool calls with asynchronous completion (e.g., long-running search)
- user-visible “undo” / “retry last step”
- replaying a full agent trace for debugging

Include what you store (messages, tool calls, intermediate thoughts if any), where you store it (cache vs DB), and how you handle consistency and privacy.
questions.correct_answer:
A robust design separates fast session state from durable trace/audit state:

1) Conversation state
- Durable store (DB): conversation metadata, message history (user/assistant), timestamps, redaction status, retention policy.
- Cache (Redis): hot window of recent turns, rendered context, and derived state (e.g., conversation summary) to reduce read latency.

2) Tool-call state
- Persist each tool call as a record: tool_name, version, input payload, idempotency key, status (pending/running/succeeded/failed), timestamps, and output.
- For async tools: a job queue + worker updates tool-call records; assistant can poll/subscribe.

3) Idempotency and retries
- Generate idempotency key per tool call (e.g., hash of conversation_id + step_id + tool_name + normalized args) and store it.
- On retry, check existing record; if succeeded, reuse output; if running, return “in progress”; if failed, decide policy.

4) Undo/retry
- Model the agent execution as a step graph (trace spans). “Undo” creates a new branch/version of the trace rather than mutating history.
- Keep deterministic replay inputs: prompt version, model version, tool versions, and retrieved context snapshots.

5) Replay/debugging
- Trace store: span model with parent/child relationships (LLM call, retrieval, tool call). Store inputs/outputs, latency, token usage, and errors.
- Redact sensitive fields; optionally store encrypted payloads with strict access controls.

6) Privacy/consistency
- Apply PII classification at ingestion; redact logs; encrypt at rest.
- Retention policies per data type (messages vs traces vs embeddings). Ensure deletion cascades across message store, trace store, and tool-call records.

This supports low-latency chats, safe retries, and reproducible debugging without leaking sensitive data.
questions.explanation: Conversational systems need both performance and correctness under retries. Durable state enables recovery and auditability; caching improves latency. Modeling tool calls explicitly with idempotency keys prevents duplicate side effects. A trace/span representation is the practical foundation for replay, branching (undo), and root-cause analysis.

questions.question_text:
Multiple choice: In an agentic LLM system that calls tools (payments, CRM, ticketing), which approach best ensures *idempotent* tool execution and supports replay/debugging?

Assume retries can happen due to timeouts and users may refresh the page.
options: A. Store the entire conversation only in the client to avoid server-side state
B. Add a random delay between retries to reduce duplicate tool calls
C. Use a server-side trace store with per-tool-call idempotency keys and persist tool inputs/outputs linked to a trace/span model
D. Disable retries and fail fast so duplicate tool calls never happen
questions.explanation: Idempotency requires the server/tool layer to recognize repeated requests and return the same result without re-executing side effects. A trace store that persists tool-call inputs/outputs and ties them to an idempotency key enables both safe retries and replay/debugging of agent traces. Client-only state and retry delays don’t guarantee correctness; disabling retries harms reliability.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an LLM app where prompts change frequently and are A/B tested. The model version changes less often.

Design a *prompt/version management* system distinct from model versioning. Include:
- a prompt registry (what metadata is stored?)
- rollout and experimentation (traffic splitting, guardrails, rollback)
- compatibility with tools/function calling (schemas, contracts)
- prompt evaluation pipeline (offline evals + online metrics)

Provide a high-level API and data flow.
questions.correct_answer:
A practical prompt management system treats prompts as first-class, versioned artifacts with their own lifecycle:

1) Prompt registry
- Store: prompt template text, variables, tool/function schemas, system instructions, constraints, few-shot examples, and output format requirements.
- Metadata: owner, description, tags/use-case, safety classification, required tools, expected JSON schema, supported model families, creation timestamp, changelog.
- Versioning: immutable versions (e.g., prompt_id + version), with “aliases” like production/staging.

2) Rollout/experimentation
- A routing layer that selects prompt version by experiment assignment (user_id hash) and environment.
- Guardrails: safety filters, schema validation, max token limits, allowlist of tools.
- Rollback: flip alias back to previous version; keep audit trail.

3) Tool compatibility
- Treat tool schemas as contracts: version tools independently and declare prompt->tool compatibility.
- Validate outputs against JSON schema; enforce function-calling signatures.
- Integration tests that run prompt versions against mocked tools.

4) Evaluation pipelines (distinct from model eval)
- Offline: curated eval set per use-case; run prompt versions across representative inputs; score with task metrics (exact match, rubric/LLM-judge, schema validity, tool-call correctness, cost/latency).
- Online: log prompt version, tool calls, user feedback, success/failure outcomes; compute metrics by segment.
- Promotion rules: require passing offline thresholds + canary online metrics.

5) APIs/data flow
- Prompt service API: GET /prompts/{id}?version=, POST /prompts, POST /aliases/{name} to point to a version, GET /experiments/{id}.
- Inference gateway: resolves prompt alias -> concrete version -> renders template -> calls model -> validates output -> executes tools.
- Telemetry pipeline: structured logs keyed by prompt_version + model_version + tool_version.

This separates prompt iteration from model release, enabling fast safe changes and reproducible debugging.
questions.explanation: In LLM products, prompt changes are frequent and can break tool calling or output formats. Treating prompts as versioned artifacts with compatibility metadata and evaluation gates prevents silent regressions. Separating prompt and model versioning allows independent rollouts, reproducibility, and clear attribution when metrics change.

questions.question_text:
Multiple choice: Which design choice most directly prevents training/serving skew when using a feature store?

Assume you already have an online store (low latency) and an offline store (historical).
options: A. Use the same database technology for both online and offline stores
B. Define features in a versioned registry/DSL and generate both offline backfills and online materialization/lookup code from the same definition
C. Increase online store TTL so features are rarely missing at serving time
D. Store all features as embeddings to make them model-agnostic
questions.explanation: Skew typically comes from *different computation paths* (different code, different joins, different windows) rather than different storage engines. A versioned feature definition (DSL/spec) that drives both offline and online computation ensures the same semantics and reduces drift caused by hand-coded pipelines. TTL and storage choice matter for availability/perf, not semantic parity.

questions.question_text:
You’re building a feature store for multiple teams (fraud, growth, search). The main problem is not storage choice but preventing training/serving skew and enabling reuse.

Design the *feature definition and computation* approach. What would you standardize (e.g., DSL, transformation library, compute engine), and how would you enforce:
- point-in-time correctness
- online/offline parity
- ownership and reuse governance
- feature discovery

Describe the key components and the data flow at a high level.
questions.correct_answer:
A strong design standardizes feature computation as a single source of truth, not just where features are stored. Typical components:

1) Feature registry + definitions (DSL/spec)
- A declarative feature spec (YAML/Proto/SQL-like DSL) describing: entities/keys, sources, transformations, windows, freshness/TTL, and labels/metadata (owner, SLA, PII classification).
- Versioned definitions with review/approval.

2) Standardized transformation library
- Shared, tested transforms (e.g., time-window aggregates, normalization, bucketing) with deterministic behavior.
- Avoid ad-hoc notebook logic; require transforms to be expressed in the DSL or a vetted library.

3) Unified computation for offline and online
- Compute features once via the same logic: either (a) compile DSL to both batch (Spark) and streaming (Flink) jobs, or (b) compute in batch and materialize to online with identical semantics.
- For online, precompute or incremental update; for offline, backfill with point-in-time joins.

4) Point-in-time correctness
- Store event time and ingestion time; enforce time-travel joins against observation time.
- Feature specs include window definitions relative to the observation timestamp.
- A “training set builder” that automatically performs point-in-time joins and prevents leakage.

5) Governance + ownership
- Registry metadata: owner team, on-call, documentation, data contract, PII classification.
- Approval workflow for promoted features; deprecation policy; automated tests (schema checks, distribution checks, parity tests).
- Access control by feature/namespace.

6) Discovery and reuse
- Searchable catalog with tags, entities, examples, lineage, and usage stats.
- Recommend existing features by entity and similarity.

7) Skew prevention enforcement
- Automated offline-vs-online parity checks on sampled traffic.
- “Feature views” compiled from the same spec; disallow custom transformations in serving path.
- Monitoring for freshness, null rate, distribution shift; alert owners.

Data flow: raw sources -> standardized ETL/streaming -> feature computation jobs generated from definitions -> offline store (training) + online store (serving) materialization -> training set builder and online feature retrieval both use the same registry/spec, ensuring parity.
questions.explanation: Feature stores fail in practice when teams compute features differently in training vs serving. A definition/registry + standardized computation layer (DSL/transforms) creates a single source of truth, enabling point-in-time correctness, parity, governance, and discovery. Enforcing parity via compilation, tests, and disallowing ad-hoc serving transforms addresses the most common skew root cause.

questions.question_text:
You’re exposing an LLM inference API to multiple external enterprise tenants. Requirements:
- Each tenant has its own API keys and usage quota
- Some tenants require strict data isolation (no cross-tenant caching of prompts/responses)
- Prompts may contain PII; logs must be safe
- You must mitigate abuse (prompt injection attempts, high-rate scraping, toxic content)

Describe the security design: authn/authz, tenant isolation, secrets management, PII handling in logs/prompts, and abuse/rate limiting controls.
questions.correct_answer: A strong design includes: Authn via per-tenant API keys or OAuth2 client credentials; authz enforcing tenant_id scoping on every request and resource (model access, max tokens, tools). Tenant isolation: separate namespaces/projects or logical partitioning with strict row-level security; avoid shared caches for sensitive tenants or key caches by (tenant_id, model_id); consider dedicated deployments for highest isolation. Secrets management: store keys in a secrets manager (KMS-backed), rotate regularly, never embed in images; short-lived tokens for internal service-to-service auth (mTLS/JWT). PII handling: minimize retention, redact/tokenize PII in logs, configurable logging levels, encryption at rest/in transit, data residency controls, and explicit consent for storing prompts; ensure prompts/responses don’t get sent to third parties without contract. Abuse controls: per-tenant and per-IP rate limits, quota/budget enforcement, anomaly detection, content moderation/safety filters, prompt injection defenses (tool allowlists, sandboxing, least-privilege tool access), request size limits, and audit logs. Include incident response: revoke keys, isolate tenant, and runbooks.
questions.explanation: This checks ML/LLM security beyond basics: multi-tenant authz, isolation and caching pitfalls, secrets hygiene, safe logging with PII, and abuse controls specific to LLM endpoints (prompt injection, tool misuse, scraping).

questions.question_text:
A producer team wants to change an event schema used to compute real-time features. They plan to:
- rename field `user_id` -> `uid`
- change `amount` from string to integer cents
- add a new nested object `device`

Your feature pipelines (streaming + offline) and online inference depend on the event.

Design a practical data contract + schema evolution strategy that avoids breaking production. Include:
- compatibility rules (backward/forward)
- validation gates (CI/CD, runtime)
- migration plan (dual-write, backfill, cutover)
- how you handle versioning for training data vs serving.
questions.correct_answer: A good strategy uses explicit schema versioning (e.g., protobuf/avro with a schema registry) and a data contract defining compatibility. Renames and type changes are breaking; handle via additive changes first: keep `user_id` while adding `uid` (dual fields) and add `amount_cents` alongside `amount` rather than changing type in-place. Add `device` as optional (additive) with defaults. Validation gates: CI checks for compatibility against registry, and consumer contract tests; runtime validation with dead-letter queues for invalid events plus metrics on violations. Migration: dual-write (produce both old and new fields), update consumers to read new fields with fallback to old, backfill offline tables to populate new columns, then cut over and finally deprecate old fields after an agreed window. For training vs serving, ensure point-in-time correctness and keep dataset generation pinned to a schema version; record schema/model/feature definitions in metadata so you can reproduce training and debug serving mismatches.
questions.explanation: This tests real-world schema evolution: the safe path is additive changes + dual write/read, not in-place breaking changes. It also checks that candidates think about both streaming and offline consumers, validation gates, and reproducibility for training data.

questions.question_text: You own an ML ranking service in production. Latency is stable, but business metrics dropped and you suspect data/model issues. Which monitoring and oncall approach is most effective?
options: A. Only monitor infrastructure metrics (CPU/GPU, memory). If they look fine, assume the model is fine.
B. Monitor p50/p95 latency and error rate only; if they are within SLO, avoid paging to reduce noise.

C. Define SLIs/SLOs that include both serving health (latency/errors) and ML health (feature freshness, missing feature rate, prediction distribution drift, calibration proxies, segment-level metrics). Use runbooks that correlate data pipeline, feature store, and model version; tune alerts with burn-rate and anomaly thresholds.
D. Page on any change in prediction distribution because any drift is always a production incident.
questions.explanation: ML systems can be ‘up’ but wrong. Effective monitoring includes ML-specific signals (feature freshness, null rates, schema violations, drift, segment regressions) and ties them to operational runbooks that help an oncall engineer localize the fault (data ingestion, feature computation, model artifact, routing). Alert tuning (burn-rate, seasonality-aware thresholds) avoids both missed incidents and alert fatigue.

questions.question_text:
Capacity planning exercise: You run an embedding service and a vector retrieval service.

Assumptions:
- 1,000 QPS search requests at peak.
- Each search request embeds a query (average 80 tokens) and then does vector search.
- Embedding model throughput: 2,000 tokens/sec per GPU at target latency.
- You want 40% headroom.
- Vector DB: each query does 1 ANN search over an index; each vector is 768-dim float16.
- You store 200M item vectors.

Questions:
1) Roughly how many GPUs do you need for query embeddings at peak?
2) Roughly how much raw memory is needed just to store the vectors (ignore index overhead), and what does that imply for sharding?
3) Name 3 levers to reduce cost without a large quality hit.
questions.correct_answer:
1) Tokens/sec needed = 1,000 QPS * 80 tokens = 80,000 tokens/sec. With 40% headroom, provision for 80,000 / 0.6 ≈ 133,333 tokens/sec. At 2,000 tokens/sec per GPU, GPUs ≈ 133,333 / 2,000 ≈ 66.7 → ~67 GPUs.
2) Vector size = 768 dims * 2 bytes (float16) = 1,536 bytes ≈ 1.5KB/vector. For 200M vectors: 200,000,000 * 1,536 ≈ 307,200,000,000 bytes ≈ 307GB (decimal) (~286GiB). This is raw vector storage; real indexes (HNSW/IVF) add overhead, so you likely need multiple shards/nodes, potentially TBs of RAM depending on index type/replication.
3) Cost levers: reduce tokens via prompt/query truncation; cache embeddings for repeated queries; use a smaller/faster embedding model or distill/quantize; batch embeddings; move some traffic to CPU if latency allows; reduce vector dimensionality; use PQ/quantization in the vector index; adjust recall/efSearch; apply prefilters to reduce search work; use tiered storage (hot subset in RAM).
questions.explanation: This tests whether candidates can do back-of-the-envelope sizing and connect it to design decisions. The key is converting QPS→tokens/sec→GPUs with headroom, computing vector memory footprint, and identifying practical cost levers across model, caching, batching, and index tuning.

questions.question_text:
You’re computing real-time ML features for fraud detection, including:
- count of transactions per card in the last 5 minutes
- sum of spend per merchant in the last 1 hour
- join transactions with a stream of card status updates (active/blocked)

Events can arrive up to 2 minutes late. You serve features online with low latency.

How would you design the streaming computation? Include:
- windowing strategy
- joins (stream-stream or stream-table)
- watermarking / late data handling
- how features are materialized for online serving
- how you ensure training/serving consistency.
questions.correct_answer: A solid design uses a streaming engine (e.g., Flink/Spark Structured Streaming/Kafka Streams) with keyed aggregations. Use sliding/tumbling windows for counts/sums (e.g., 5-min sliding updated every N seconds; 1-hour window), keyed by card_id or merchant_id. Card status updates are best modeled as a changelog stream materialized into a keyed state store (stream-table join) so each transaction joins against the latest status at event time; if true event-time correctness is required, maintain versioned status with event-time semantics. Use watermarks with allowed lateness of ~2 minutes; late events within allowed lateness update windowed aggregates; beyond that, either drop, route to a correction pipeline, or emit compensating updates depending on risk tolerance. Materialize features into an online store (KV/feature store) with TTL aligned to window sizes; publish feature updates with idempotent upserts. For training/serving consistency, reuse the same feature definitions (feature repo), compute offline features from the same raw event logs with the same window specs, and validate parity (backtests, point-in-time joins) to avoid leakage.
questions.explanation: This checks ML-specific streaming design: correct event-time windows for features, handling late/out-of-order data, and join patterns that avoid leakage. It also tests whether candidates think about feature materialization (TTL, idempotency) and training/serving consistency rather than only generic streaming terms.

questions.question_text:
You need approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search over 200M embeddings (768-dim float16) with:
- p95 query latency < 80ms
- frequent incremental updates (new items every minute)
- hybrid retrieval: filter by language + category, then ANN
- multi-tenant isolation (some tenants have private items)

Compare when you would choose:
1) a managed vector database
2) an ANN library embedded in your service (e.g., FAISS/HNSWlib)
3) a search engine with vector support

State key tradeoffs (ops burden, filtering, updates, cost, isolation, failure modes) and your likely choice here.
questions.correct_answer: A good comparison: (1) Managed vector DB reduces ops and provides sharding/replication, ingestion pipelines, metadata filtering, and sometimes multi-tenancy controls; tradeoffs are cost, vendor constraints, and less control over index internals. (2) Embedded ANN library offers maximum control and potentially best cost/perf per node, but you must build sharding, replication, rebalancing, backups, rolling index rebuilds, and query routing; filtering and multi-tenant isolation become your responsibility. (3) Search engine with vector support is strong for hybrid retrieval (BM25 + vector, rich filters), mature ops, and multi-tenant patterns, but vector performance/recall may lag specialized vector DBs at extreme scale, and index tuning can be complex. For 200M vectors with frequent updates + hybrid filters + multi-tenancy, many would pick a vector DB or search engine depending on how critical hybrid retrieval is; if strict hybrid + mature filtering dominates, a search engine is compelling; if pure vector performance/recall and update throughput dominate, a vector DB is likely best. The answer should mention index type (HNSW/IVF/PQ), memory footprint, and update vs rebuild tradeoffs.
questions.explanation: This tests practical storage/system selection, not trivia. The key is connecting requirements (scale, updates, filtering, tenancy) to operational and performance tradeoffs, and demonstrating you know what you’d have to build yourself if you pick an embedded library.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an ML feature platform. Which storage choice is the best default for each need?

1) Transactional writes/reads for online features per user with low latency
2) Large-scale offline training datasets with heavy scans/aggregations
3) Storing raw immutable data dumps (images, parquet, logs) cheaply

Pick the best mapping.
options: A. (1) Object storage, (2) OLTP DB, (3) OLAP warehouse
B. (1) OLTP DB / low-latency KV store, (2) OLAP warehouse/lakehouse, (3) Object storage
C. (1) OLAP warehouse, (2) Object storage, (3) OLTP DB
D. (1) Search engine, (2) OLTP DB, (3) Vector database
questions.explanation: Online features typically need low-latency point lookups and frequent updates → OLTP/KV (e.g., Redis/Cassandra/DynamoDB). Offline training workloads are scan/aggregate heavy → OLAP warehouse/lakehouse (e.g., BigQuery/Snowflake/Spark on lake). Raw immutable data is cheapest and most flexible in object storage (S3/GCS/Azure Blob).

questions.question_text:
Design a model loading and caching strategy for an embedding service where:
- You serve multiple embedding models (versions) and tenants
- Models range from 500MB to 12GB
- You deploy on Kubernetes with GPU nodes
- You need fast rollouts and minimal cold starts

Include:
- cache hierarchy (e.g., object store, node disk, RAM, GPU memory)
- lazy vs eager loading tradeoffs
- precomputing embeddings (when it helps)
- how you avoid cache stampedes during deploys.
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer proposes a multi-level cache: model artifacts in object storage (source of truth) + optional CDN; node-level persistent disk cache (e.g., hostPath/daemonset prefetch) to avoid repeated downloads; container/RAM cache for weights; and GPU memory residency for active models. Eager loading improves first-request latency but increases startup time and resource use; lazy loading reduces baseline cost but risks tail latency spikes—often mitigated by warming the top-N models per node and lazy-loading long-tail models. Precomputing embeddings helps when documents/items change slowly and queries need fast retrieval (store embeddings in vector index); it doesn’t help for truly ad-hoc user text unless you cache repeated inputs. Avoid stampedes via rollout waves, prewarm jobs, request coalescing (single-flight) per (model_id, version), and rate limits on artifact downloads; also pin versions and use canaries.
questions.explanation: This probes practical inference warmup: candidates should reason about where bytes move (object store → disk → RAM → GPU), what to keep hot, and how to keep deploys safe (stampede control). It also checks understanding of when embedding precompute is valuable (static corpora) vs not (unique user inputs).

questions.question_text: You deploy a GPU inference service and see frequent latency spikes when new pods start. The model is 8GB and takes ~45s to load from remote storage. Which approach most directly reduces cold-start impact while keeping utilization reasonable?
options: A. Disable autoscaling so pods never start during traffic; accept occasional overload.
B. Maintain a warm pool of ready pods with the model already loaded; scale by swapping warm pods into service and replenishing the pool asynchronously.
C. Increase request timeout from 2s to 60s so cold starts complete without errors.
D. Move tokenization to the client so the server does less work during startup.
questions.explanation: A warm pool (or pre-warmed nodes/containers) is a standard cold-start mitigation for large model artifacts. It decouples scaling events from model load time and stabilizes latency. Disabling autoscaling harms reliability, increasing timeouts hides the issue and hurts UX, and client-side tokenization doesn’t address model artifact load time.

questions.question_text:
Design a cost-aware dynamic model selection system for an LLM-backed endpoint (e.g., /chat). You have:
- Small model: cheap, fast, weaker
- Large model: expensive, slower, better

Requirements:
- Meet a monthly spend cap
- Maintain p95 latency under 1.5s for 90% of requests
- Paid tier should rarely be downgraded
- You can use request metadata (tenant, intent, length, safety risk) and online signals (queue depth, error rates)

Describe:
1) what signals you would use for routing
2) the policy/algorithm (rules, bandit, optimization)
3) how you would enforce spend caps (budgeting)
4) how you would evaluate impact (offline + online)
5) failure modes and safeguards.
questions.correct_answer: A good design uses a routing service that computes a per-request decision using: tenant/tier, prompt length/token estimate, intent classification, safety risk, expected value (business), current system load (GPU queue depth), model health, and remaining budget (global + per-tenant). Policy can start as rule-based (tier gates + max tokens thresholds + load shedding) and evolve to a contextual bandit or constrained optimization (maximize quality subject to latency and cost constraints). Spend caps enforced via token accounting, budgets that reset monthly, per-tenant quotas, and a real-time budget ledger; when nearing cap, progressively shift marginal traffic to smaller model or reduce max tokens. Evaluation: offline replay with logged prompts and quality proxies, plus online A/B with guardrails (quality metrics, CSAT, escalation rate, safety). Safeguards: circuit breakers, deterministic fallback rules, per-tenant isolation, rate limits, prompt truncation, abuse detection, and ensuring routing decisions are explainable/auditable.
questions.explanation: This tests practical routing beyond ‘pick a model’: you need a policy that simultaneously manages quality, latency, and cost under budget constraints, and you must plan for measurement, guardrails, and operational safety (health checks, circuit breakers, auditability).

questions.question_text:
Your company serves predictions for multiple customer segments (free tier vs paid tier). You have two models:
- Model A: large, higher quality, higher cost/latency
- Model B: smaller, lower quality, cheaper

You want: paid users get Model A by default; free users get Model B; during incidents you can dynamically shift traffic; and you need a safe fallback if Model A is unhealthy.

Which design best matches these needs?
options: A. Hardcode a segment->model mapping in the inference code and redeploy to change behavior; fallback by returning HTTP 500 if Model A fails.
B. Use a routing layer with segment-based rules + feature-flag/config service; add health-based fallback to Model B; support gradual traffic shifting via weighted routing.
C. Put both models behind a single endpoint and let the client choose the model via a query parameter; rely on client retries for fallback.
D. Always run Model A first; if it times out, run Model B; make no distinction between segments to keep the system simple.
questions.explanation: A routing layer (or gateway) that consults segment attributes plus a dynamic config/feature-flag system supports per-segment routing and rapid changes without redeploys. Health-based fallback (circuit breaker) keeps availability high when Model A is degraded. Weighted routing enables safe rollouts and incident traffic shaping. The other options either push responsibility to clients, require redeploys, or waste cost by always attempting the large model.

questions.question_text:
You’re asked to design an online inference service for a text classification model (e.g., toxicity detection) that will be called by multiple internal products.

As an ML engineer (not a system design specialist), walk through:
1) requirements gathering (functional + non-functional)
2) an API sketch (request/response)
3) a minimal data model (what you log/store)
4) a high-level architecture
5) likely bottlenecks and 2-3 key tradeoffs you would articulate.

Be concrete (latency targets, scale assumptions, what you would measure).
questions.correct_answer: A strong answer starts by clarifying functional requirements (single/batch inference, supported languages, model versioning, explainability outputs, thresholding, async vs sync) and non-functional requirements (p95/p99 latency, QPS, availability, consistency, privacy/PII, cost, multi-tenancy, observability). It proposes an API (e.g., POST /v1/classify with text, metadata, optional model_hint; response with label/prob, model_version, request_id). Minimal data model includes request_id, timestamps, tenant/app, model_version, features hash, prediction, latency breakdown, error codes, and carefully controlled raw text storage (often not stored; or stored with consent/redaction). Architecture: API gateway + auth, rate limiting, request validation, routing layer, inference service (GPU/CPU), model registry + artifact store, feature retrieval (if any), logging/metrics pipeline, canary/rollout mechanism. Bottlenecks: model load/cold start, GPU saturation, tokenization CPU, network hops, feature store latency, vectorization, logging backpressure. Tradeoffs: sync vs async, batch vs single, CPU vs GPU, caching vs freshness, storing raw inputs vs privacy, per-tenant isolation vs efficiency, strict schema validation vs agility.
questions.explanation: This tests core ML-oriented system design fundamentals: you should show you can translate ambiguous product needs into measurable SLOs, define a workable API, decide what to persist for debugging/monitoring, and propose a simple deployable architecture. The key is being explicit about bottlenecks (tokenization, model execution, feature fetch, cold starts) and tradeoffs (latency vs cost, privacy vs debuggability, isolation vs utilization).

questions.question_text:
Your model serving stack depends on: (1) an online feature store, (2) a vector search service for retrieval, and (3) a large LLM endpoint. You’re seeing partial outages in the vector search service.

Design resiliency strategies specific to ML serving:
- Timeouts and fallbacks (e.g., smaller model, cached retrieval, heuristic response)
- Circuit breakers and bulkheads (per dependency and per tenant)
- Degraded modes and response semantics (what do you return?)
- How you would test these failure modes (chaos / fault injection)
- What to monitor and alert on

Be explicit about tradeoffs (quality vs latency vs cost).
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer: set strict per-dependency timeouts and an overall request deadline; implement circuit breakers for vector search (open on high error/latency), bulkheads to isolate tenants and prevent one dependency from exhausting threads/GPU slots. Fallbacks: (a) serve from cached retrieval results with TTL, (b) use a smaller/cheaper model or a non-RAG prompt when retrieval fails, (c) return partial results with a ‘retrieval_unavailable’ flag, or (d) degrade to a heuristic baseline. Define response semantics so clients can detect degraded answers.

Add backpressure: limit concurrent retrieval calls; shed load with 429/503. Use retries only when safe (idempotent, within budget) and avoid retry storms.

Testing: fault injection to add latency/errors to vector search, simulate partial outages, verify circuit breaker behavior and SLA adherence; run game days.

Monitoring: dependency-specific success rate/latency, circuit breaker state, fallback rate, cache hit rate, quality proxies (e.g., human rating sampling, answer length, escalation), and cost per request. Tradeoff discussion: fallbacks protect availability/latency but may reduce answer quality; caching improves latency but risks staleness; smaller model reduces cost but may hurt accuracy.
questions.explanation: ML serving often has multiple downstream dependencies, and outages manifest as quality regressions as well as errors. Interviewers want to see practical resiliency tools (timeouts, circuit breakers, bulkheads, fallbacks) and an understanding of how to communicate degraded responses and monitor quality impact—not just ‘add retries.’

questions.question_text:
Multiple Choice: You maintain a training dataset built from multiple event streams. A bug in feature computation was discovered, and you need to rebuild the last 90 days of training data.

Which pipeline approach best supports correct reprocessing and lineage?
options: A) Overwrite today’s dataset in place; older versions are not needed if the latest is correct
B) Keep immutable raw data (or snapshots), run a versioned transformation job to produce a new dataset version, and record a manifest linking inputs/code/version to outputs
C) Only reprocess the most recent day; the model will generalize to older periods
D) Manually patch the existing dataset by editing rows where the bug is suspected
questions.explanation: Reliable reprocessing requires immutable inputs (or reproducible snapshots), versioned code/config, and explicit lineage (a manifest) so you can explain what changed and reproduce the dataset later. Overwriting in place (A) destroys auditability. Partial rebuilds (C) may leave systematic bias. Manual patching (D) is error-prone and not scalable.

questions.question_text:
You are implementing A/B testing for a model change in production.

Scenario:
- You have an existing model (control) and a new model (treatment)
- You can only observe delayed labels (e.g., conversion happens hours later)
- You need guardrails to prevent bad user impact

Design:
1) Traffic allocation and assignment (sticky bucketing, unit of randomization)
2) Online metrics vs offline metrics you would track
3) Guardrail metrics and rollback criteria
4) How to handle delayed labels and avoid peeking/false positives
5) Common pitfalls (sample ratio mismatch, interference, novelty effects)
questions.correct_answer:
A strong answer: (1) Use consistent hashing for sticky assignment at the right unit (user_id/account) to avoid contamination; store assignment and model revision in logs. (2) Online metrics: latency, error rate, cost per request, engagement proxies; Offline/label-based: conversion/retention with proper attribution windows. (3) Guardrails: p95 latency, 5xx rate, safety/toxicity, escalation rate, CTR drop thresholds; define automated rollback triggers and manual review. (4) Delayed labels: predefine analysis windows, use sequential testing or holdout windows, avoid repeated significance checks; use CUPED or stratification to reduce variance; ensure logging supports joining exposures to outcomes. (5) Pitfalls: sample ratio mismatch (instrumentation/assignment bugs), interference/network effects, caching causing cross-variant leakage, novelty effects and learning-to-rank feedback loops; ensure experiment isolation and correct attribution.

Include discussion of canary rollout before full A/B, and monitoring dashboards keyed by experiment_id.
questions.explanation: Production evaluation is a system problem: correct randomization, logging, delayed outcomes, and guardrails determine whether you can trust results and protect users. Interviewers look for awareness of assignment stickiness, attribution windows, and operational rollback—not just ‘run an A/B test.’

questions.question_text:
Multiple Choice: Your synchronous inference endpoint sometimes experiences overload. You want to protect GPUs from queue buildup and prevent cascading failures.

Which combination is most appropriate?
options: A) Increase the HTTP server thread pool size and remove timeouts so requests eventually finish
B) Add request batching, enforce max in-flight requests per replica, and return 429/503 with Retry-After when saturated
C) Disable batching and accept all requests; autoscaling will catch up
D) Route all traffic to a single large GPU to maximize utilization
questions.explanation: Backpressure is essential in inference systems. Batching improves throughput, concurrency limits prevent unbounded queueing (which drives p99/p999 up), and explicit overload responses (429/503 + Retry-After) keep the system stable and allow clients to retry safely. Removing timeouts (A) and accepting all requests (C) cause queue buildup and tail-latency collapse. A single large GPU (D) is a reliability and scaling bottleneck.

questions.question_text:
You’re asked in an interview to design a real-time ranking service that uses an online feature store and a model server.

In 10–15 minutes, how would you communicate the design?
Include:
- Clear component boundaries (what services exist and what they own)
- API contracts between components (request/response + timeouts)
- Data contracts (schemas, feature freshness expectations)
- High-level sizing (QPS, latency budget, rough capacity)
- Key tradeoffs and failure modes

Write the outline you would present verbally (bullet points are fine).
questions.correct_answer:
A strong outline: (1) Requirements: QPS, p95/p99 latency, availability, freshness, cost, model update cadence. (2) Components: API gateway; ranking orchestrator; feature fetcher/online feature store; model inference service; cache; logging/metrics/tracing; config/model registry; offline pipeline for features/training. Define ownership boundaries explicitly. (3) Contracts: ranking request includes user_id/context/items; feature store GetFeatures(entity_ids, feature_set_version) with strict timeout; inference Predict(model_revision, feature_vector_batch) with timeout and max batch size; responses include request_id, model_revision, feature_set_version. (4) Data contracts: feature schemas, TTL/freshness SLA, default/imputation rules, point-in-time semantics for training, versioning for feature sets. (5) Sizing: latency budget allocation (e.g., 20ms feature fetch, 30ms inference, 10ms overhead), QPS to GPU count via throughput per GPU and batching, caches for hot entities, autoscaling triggers. (6) Tradeoffs/failures: cache staleness vs latency; eventual consistency in feature store; fallback ranking when inference fails; circuit breakers; rate limiting; backpressure; safe rollout (A/B or canary) and monitoring.

The key is a crisp decomposition + explicit contracts + budgeted latency/capacity and clear tradeoffs.
questions.explanation: Many ML system design interviews evaluate communication as much as architecture. Interviewers look for explicit boundaries, contracts, and budgets rather than vague boxes. A good candidate can allocate latency, specify timeouts, and articulate tradeoffs (freshness vs latency, cost vs accuracy) and resiliency behavior.

questions.question_text:
Multiple Choice: You need strong traceability for an LLM-powered support agent. For any given user-visible answer, you must later answer: which model weights, prompt template, retrieved documents, and feature values were used?

Which design choice best supports end-to-end provenance with minimal coupling?
options: A) Only log the final generated text to reduce storage costs
B) Store a single “model_version” string in application logs; other details can be inferred later
C) Emit a structured prediction record with immutable identifiers for model revision, prompt template version, retrieval query + document IDs/versions, feature set version, and trace IDs
D) Rely on the vector database’s internal logs as the source of truth for what was retrieved
questions.explanation: Provenance requires capturing immutable identifiers for every dependency used to produce an output. A structured record (often to an audit log store) decouples the serving path from later investigations and enables reproducible replays. Logging only the output (A) or a single model string (B) is insufficient. Depending on a single subsystem’s logs (D) is brittle and incomplete because the answer depends on more than retrieval.

questions.question_text:
You have both offline (training) and online (serving) feature pipelines. A model’s online performance drops, and you suspect training/serving skew.

Describe how you would design for and debug consistency between offline and online features, including:
- Feature definitions as code and versioning
- Backfills and point-in-time correctness
- Data validation checks (schema + distribution)
- How to reconcile differences when online features use TTLs or late-arriving events
- What metadata you would log at inference time to enable root-cause analysis
questions.correct_answer:
A good answer includes: (1) Centralized feature definitions (repo/registry) with versioned transformations and explicit time semantics; generate both offline and online pipelines from the same definition where possible. (2) Offline training sets built with point-in-time joins to avoid leakage; backfills are reproducible with immutable raw inputs and job manifests. (3) Validation: schema checks, null rates, freshness, and distribution drift checks comparing offline vs online feature distributions; alerts when divergence exceeds thresholds. (4) TTL/late data: define freshness SLAs per feature; for online TTL features, training should simulate the same availability (e.g., drop or impute when stale); handle late events via retractions/updates and backfill strategies, and document whether online is eventually consistent. (5) Inference logging: log feature values (or hashes), feature set version, entity id, event time, feature timestamps/freshness, model version, and request_id/trace_id; store enough lineage to reconstruct what the model saw without leaking sensitive data.

Debugging steps: compare sampled online feature vectors to offline recomputation for the same entity+time; isolate which features diverge; check TTL expiry patterns, missingness, and late event handling.
questions.explanation: Training/serving skew is one of the most common real-world failure modes. The system-design challenge is to make feature computation reproducible and comparable across offline and online paths, and to log enough metadata to trace a bad prediction back to a specific feature definition/version and data timestamp. Point-in-time correctness and TTL/late-data semantics are usually where hidden bugs live.

questions.question_text:
Multiple Choice: You’re operating an online feature store (used at inference time) and notice high latency and occasional throttling. Metrics show a single feature key is requested by a large fraction of traffic (a “hot key”).

Which mitigation is most appropriate for reducing hot-key impact while keeping feature values reasonably fresh?
options: A) Increase the offline feature store cluster size; online latency will improve automatically
B) Add a small in-process or edge cache with short TTL and request coalescing for that key
C) Disable TTLs so the value is always served from cache
D) Switch the online store from key-value to a columnar data warehouse
questions.explanation: Hot keys overload the online store partition/replica serving that key. A short-TTL cache (and request coalescing to avoid stampedes) reduces read pressure while keeping values reasonably fresh. Scaling offline storage (A) doesn’t address online read path. Disabling TTL (C) risks serving stale features. A warehouse (D) is typically higher latency and not designed for high-QPS online lookups.

questions.question_text:
You’re building an asynchronous inference API for long-running LLM tasks (e.g., document summarization over 200 pages). Clients submit a job and later fetch results.

Design the system components and data flows:
- API endpoints (submit, get status, get result, cancel)
- Queue/worker model (at-least-once delivery, concurrency limits)
- Job status store schema and state machine
- Deduplication strategy (idempotency keys) and how retries behave
- DLQ policy and what gets logged/alerted
- Callback/webhook delivery (signing, retries, ordering)

Provide a concise architecture and key tradeoffs.
questions.correct_answer:
A solid design includes: (1) Submit endpoint returns job_id immediately (202 Accepted) plus status_url; supports Idempotency-Key to dedupe submits. (2) A durable queue (SQS/Kafka/PubSub) holds job messages; workers pull with visibility timeout/lease; concurrency is limited per GPU/tenant; implement backpressure via queue depth and per-tenant quotas. (3) Status store (Dynamo/Postgres) with fields: job_id, tenant_id, idempotency_key, request_hash, state (QUEUED/RUNNING/SUCCEEDED/FAILED/CANCELED), timestamps, attempt_count, worker_id, result_pointer (e.g., S3 URI), error_code/error_message, trace_ids. (4) Dedup: on submit, store (tenant_id, idempotency_key)->job_id with TTL; if same key+same hash, return existing job_id; if same key different hash -> 409. Worker retries are safe because processing is idempotent: write results with conditional put and/or use job state transitions with compare-and-swap. (5) DLQ after N attempts or non-retryable error; alert on DLQ rate; capture minimal PII; store failure reason and correlation ids. (6) Webhooks: store callback URL + secret, sign payload (HMAC), include event_id for idempotency, retry with exponential backoff, stop after max attempts, allow client to re-fetch via GET result; avoid strict ordering guarantees unless specified; include status transitions in events.

Tradeoffs: at-least-once requires idempotent workers; webhook delivery is best-effort so GET status/result is the source of truth; visibility timeouts must exceed expected processing or use heartbeats/leases.
questions.explanation: Async inference is common for LLM workloads where runtime is variable and can exceed typical HTTP timeouts. The key system-design skills are: durable job submission, at-least-once processing, idempotent retries, a clear job state machine, and operational safety via DLQs and alerting. Webhooks introduce their own reliability problems, so a robust design treats them as notifications while keeping the status store as the authoritative record.

questions.question_text:
Multiple Choice: You operate a GPU-backed inference service that supports multiple models per GPU node. You want to reduce p99 latency spikes caused by model loading.

Which approach most directly reduces cold-start latency while keeping GPU utilization high?
options: A) Disable autoscaling and keep a fixed number of GPU instances running at all times
